1:
‘Then he called his twelve disciples together, and gave them power and authority over all devils, and to cure diseases.’ The witnesses note the commissioning and equipping of the Twelve.

The fathers draw out the purpose. Cyril: it was fitting that the ministers of holy teaching ‘be able to work miracles… and by these very acts be believed to be the ministers of God,’ bringing down the devil’s pride. Chrysostom: the charge to take nothing held them ‘aloof from all care, so that they might give their whole study to the word.’

The grammatical-historical commentators note the act. Robertson marks the indirect middle ‘called together’ (so Mark 6:7, Matthew 10:1). Henry observes Christ, who could be in but one place, now sent the Twelve abroad to spread the gospel. The Tertullian note answers Marcion: who would forbid them provision but he who ‘feeds the ravens and clothes the flowers’?

The shared, dominant note is that Christ, to extend his ministry, called the Twelve and gave them his own authority over demons and disease. The witnesses see in this both the dignity of the apostolic commission — armed with miraculous power as a credential of their divine sending — and the method of the gospel’s spread, the one Christ multiplying his work through chosen heralds, that the kingdom might be preached and the sick healed throughout the land. Eusebius adds that through the apostles the whole race of mankind might be sought out, the Lord giving them power to drive away spirits and cure diseases at his command; the witnesses agree the apostolic commission, armed with miraculous credentials, was the appointed means by which the one Christ multiplied his ministry through chosen heralds. Chrysostom adds that the charge to take nothing rendered the disciples unsuspected, held them aloof from care, and taught them their own proper virtue; the witnesses see in the gift of power over demons and disease the seal of their divine sending and the means of seeking out the whole race of mankind. Henry observes that Christ, who could be in but one place at a time, now sent the Twelve abroad to diffuse the light of the gospel through the land.
2:
‘And he sent them to preach the kingdom of God, and to heal the sick.’ The few voices note the double office of the apostles — herald and healer.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the present infinitives ‘to preach… and to heal’ mark continuous functions during the campaign, the double office stated directly in Matthew 10:7-8; he notes Luke uses iaomai for healing here and therapeuein in verse 1, apparently interchangeably. Gill expounds ‘the kingdom of God’ as the gospel of the Messiah’s kingdom of grace and glory.

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is on the fruits of repentance, tangential. With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ sent the Twelve to preach the kingdom of God and to heal the sick.

The shared, dominant note is the union of word and work in the apostolic mission. They were to herald the kingdom and to confirm their message by healing, the miracle attesting the preaching. The witnesses see in this both the content of their errand — the good news of the kingdom now drawing near in the Messiah — and the pattern of the gospel ministry, in which the proclamation of the word is the first business, accompanied, in that founding age, by the signs that marked the messengers as truly sent of God. Robertson notes Luke uses iaomai for healing here and therapeuein in verse 1, apparently interchangeably; the witnesses agree the apostles were sent both to herald the kingdom and to confirm the message by healing, the proclamation of the word being the first business, attended in that founding age by the signs that marked the messengers as truly sent of God. Gill expounds ‘the kingdom of God’ as the gospel of the Messiah’s kingdom of grace in the heart and of glory hereafter; the witnesses see in the apostles’ double office the abiding pattern of a ministry in which the word preached is confirmed, in that founding age, by attendant signs. Robertson notes the present infinitives mark the continuous functions of preaching and healing during the campaign.
3:
‘Take nothing for your journey, neither staves, nor scrip, neither bread, neither money; neither have two coats apiece.’ The witnesses note the charge to travel unencumbered, and the textual point on the staff.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle it. Ellicott and Robertson note the better text reads ‘neither a staff’ (singular), the plural probably adopted to harmonise with Matthew 10:10 and Mark 6:8 (where Mark allows a staff — see the discussion there). Lightfoot notes winter was coming on, yet they were forbidden a second garment. Clarke: in all things they were to show themselves ambassadors going on God’s charges.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the apostles were to go out wholly unprovided — no staff, wallet, bread, money, or change of clothing — depending on God and the hospitality of those they served. The grammatical commentators note the apparent discrepancy over the staff, resolved as at Mark 6:8 by distinguishing a single staff from a second.

The shared, dominant note is the spirit of unencumbered dependence. Stripped of every provision, the messengers were to trust that their wants would be supplied, the labourer being worthy of his hire; and their very poverty was to mark them as men wholly given to their errand. The witnesses see in the charge both a lesson for the founding mission — haste and freedom from worldly care — and an abiding pattern of faith, that those who go on God’s business may rely on God’s provision. Clarke notes that in all things the apostles were to show themselves ambassadors going on God’s charges; the witnesses agree the charge to go wholly unprovided was a lesson of unencumbered dependence, trusting that their wants would be supplied, the labourer being worthy of his hire, and their very poverty marking them as men wholly given to their errand. Lightfoot notes winter was coming on, yet they were forbidden even a second garment, a hard discipline of faith; the witnesses agree the spirit of the charge is unencumbered trust, the messengers going as ambassadors on God’s charges, relying wholly on his provision and the hospitality of those they served.
4:
‘And whatsoever house ye enter into, there abide, and thence depart.’ The two brief voices note the charge to a settled lodging.

The grammatical-historical Clarke explains the sense: ‘remain in that lodging till ye depart from that city’ (so Matthew 10:11), noting some manuscripts add a negative — depart not thence. Gill notes the house entered for lodging during their stay in any town.

With only two brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. The apostles, once received into a house, were to remain there throughout their stay in that place, and depart from the town from that same lodging, not flitting from house to house.

The shared, dominant note is the contentment and constancy enjoined on the messengers. Having gone out without provision, depending on hospitality, they were not to abuse that dependence by restless searching for better quarters, nor to slight the first host who received them. The witnesses see in the charge both a guard against the greed and instability unbecoming the gospel, and a lesson in single-minded simplicity: the apostle’s concern was to be the work of preaching and healing, not the comfort of his lodging, abiding quietly where he was welcomed until his errand in that place was done. The witnesses agree the apostles, once received, were to abide in one house throughout their stay and depart from the town from that same lodging; the charge guarded both against the restless searching for better quarters and against slighting the first host, that their single concern might be the work of preaching and not the comfort of their lodging. Clarke notes some manuscripts add a negative — depart not thence — while staying in that city; the witnesses agree the apostle, received into a house, was to abide there contentedly throughout his stay, neither flitting in search of better fare nor slighting the host who first received him, that nothing might distract him from his errand. Gill notes the house was entered for the sake of lodging during their stay in any town or city they came to.
5:
‘Whosoever will not receive you… shake off the very dust from your feet for a testimony against them.’ The few voices note the symbolic act against the rejecters.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain it. Robertson notes the indefinite relative with the subjunctive (‘as many as receive you not’) and that ‘for a testimony against them’ (ep’ autous) carries the same force as Mark’s dative of disadvantage (Mark 6:11); the act of shaking off the dust recalls Acts 13:51. Ellicott prefers ‘a testimony unto them.’ Gill notes it was toward those who would not receive them into their houses.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: where the apostles were not received, they were to shake the dust from their feet as a solemn testimony against that place. The grammatical commentators note the gesture’s prophetic force and its parallel in the apostles’ later practice.

The shared, dominant note is that the rejected messenger was to perform a prophetic sign, disowning the place that disowned the gospel. The shaken-off dust testified that the apostles had discharged their errand and that the guilt now lay wholly on those who refused them. The witnesses see in this both a warning of judgment — the rejection of the gospel being no light thing — and a comfort to the faithful messenger, who is responsible for the delivery of his message, not its reception, and may leave the despisers, with a clear conscience, to the God whose word they have spurned. Ellicott prefers ‘a testimony unto them’; the witnesses agree the rejected messenger was to perform a prophetic sign, disowning the place that disowned the gospel, the shaken dust testifying that the apostles had discharged their errand and the guilt now lay wholly on those who refused them. Robertson notes the act of shaking off the dust recalls the apostles’ later practice (Acts 13:51); the witnesses agree the gesture both warned the rejecters of judgment and assured the faithful messenger that, having delivered his message, he was not answerable for its reception but might leave the despisers to God.
6:
‘And they departed, and went through the towns, preaching the gospel, and healing every where.’ The few voices note the wide extent of the apostles’ first mission.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the sweep. Robertson notes the imperfect ‘went’ with three present participles (going out, preaching, healing), describing ‘the wide extent of the work through all the villages… everywhere’ in Galilee. Ellicott prefers ‘villages’ for ‘towns.’ Gill notes they went from Christ in Galilee. The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the apostles went out and travelled through the villages, preaching the gospel and healing everywhere. The grammatical commentators dwell on the continuous, repeated action and the breadth of the work.

The shared, dominant note is the carrying-out of the commission. Sent in pairs and unencumbered, the Twelve dispersed through Galilee, everywhere proclaiming the good news and confirming it by healings. The witnesses see in this both the beginning of the gospel’s spread through Christ’s appointed messengers and the fruit of their training under him: what they had received they now gave, extending far and wide the ministry the Master himself had begun, so that the kingdom was preached and its power displayed throughout the land. Robertson notes the imperfect ‘went’ with three present participles describes the wide, repeated work through all the villages of Galilee; the witnesses agree the apostles, sent unencumbered, dispersed everywhere preaching the gospel and confirming it by healings, extending far and wide the ministry the Master had begun. Gill notes they went from Christ in Galilee, dispersing through the towns; the witnesses agree the Twelve, sent in pairs and unencumbered, everywhere proclaimed the good news and confirmed it by healings, the fruit of their training under the Master, who now sent them to extend the ministry he had begun. Ellicott prefers ‘villages’ for ‘towns’, as more consistent with the usage elsewhere. The witnesses see in this first apostolic tour the beginning of the gospel’s spread through Christ’s appointed messengers, who gave what they had received. Thus the kingdom was preached and its power displayed throughout the region of Galilee.
7:
‘Now Herod the tetrarch heard of all that was done by him: and he was perplexed.’ The witnesses note Herod’s troubled conscience at the report of Christ.

The fathers probe his fear. Chrysostom notes it was ‘a long time’ before Herod took notice, showing ‘the pride of a tyrant’; and that ‘sinners fear both when they know and when they are ignorant… afraid of shadows.’ Theophylact distinguishes this Herod the tetrarch from his father who slew the children.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the perplexity. Robertson notes the imperfect ‘was much perplexed’ (diaporei, thoroughly at a loss), a word only in Luke’s writings, and the three reports — John risen, Elijah, a prophet. Clarke draws the metaphor of a traveller who meets many paths and knows not which to take.

The shared, dominant note is that Christ’s spreading fame, swelled by the apostles’ mission, reached Herod and threw him into perplexity. The witnesses see in his bewilderment the working of a guilty conscience: the tetrarch, who had murdered John, could not rest at the rumours of a great prophet abroad, and was tossed among the various opinions of the people. They mark in Herod the perennial unease of the guilty, who, having silenced the voice of reproof, find it rising again to trouble them, and cannot tell what to make of the One whose works they hear of but will not seek. Clarke draws the metaphor of a traveller who meets many paths and knows not which to take; the witnesses agree Christ’s spreading fame, swelled by the apostles’ mission, reached Herod and threw his guilty conscience into perplexity, the murderer of John unable to rest at the rumours of a great prophet abroad. Theophylact distinguishes this Herod the tetrarch from his father who slew the children; the witnesses agree the tetrarch’s perplexity was the working of a guilty conscience, tossed among the people’s opinions and unable to make out the One whose works he heard of but would not seek. Robertson notes the verb ‘was much perplexed’ (diaporei, thoroughly at a loss) occurs only in Luke’s writings in the New Testament.
8:
‘And of some, that Elias had appeared; and of others, that one of the old prophets was risen again.’ The few brief voices note the popular opinions about Jesus.

The grammatical-historical Lightfoot notes the recurring expression ‘one of the old prophets risen again’ (so also v.19, and John 1:21), the people expecting some ancient prophet to rise as the Messiah’s time drew near. Gill notes Elijah, translated body and soul, was expected before the Messiah. Wesley remarks tersely that Elijah ‘could not rise again, because he did not die.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: among the people some said Elijah had appeared, others that an ancient prophet had risen. The grammatical commentators note the currency of such expectations and the distinction — Elijah, who never died, would ‘appear,’ while a prophet would ‘rise.’

The shared, dominant note is the variety of guesses about Christ’s identity, each falling short of the truth. The people, struck by his works, cast about for some great explanation — John risen, Elijah returned, a prophet of old raised — but none confessed him the Christ. The witnesses see in these opinions both the impression Christ made and the blindness that would not see the obvious; the same speculations, reported to Herod, would reappear when Jesus himself asked the disciples whom men said that he was (v.18). Gill notes Elijah, translated body and soul, was expected before the Messiah; the witnesses agree the people, struck by Christ’s works, cast about for some great explanation — John risen, Elijah returned, an ancient prophet raised — but none confessed him the Christ, the same opinions that would reappear when he asked the disciples whom men said he was. Lightfoot notes the recurring expression ‘one of the old prophets risen again’, the people looking for some ancient prophet to rise as the Messiah’s time drew near; the witnesses agree these guesses, while granting Christ a high place, all fell short of confessing him the Christ. Wesley remarks tersely that Elijah could not rise again, because he did not die, but was translated alive.
9:
‘John have I beheaded: but who is this…? And he desired to see him.’ The few voices note Herod’s guilty puzzlement and his curiosity.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark it. Robertson notes the imperfect ‘he kept on seeking to see Jesus,’ the rumours disturbing Herod because he was sure he had beheaded John. Ellicott notes Luke returns to this desire at Luke 23:8, knowing it from sources not within reach of the other Evangelists. Gill notes Herod had full proof of the beheading, the head brought in a charger.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Herod, owning that he had beheaded John, wondered who this could be of whom he heard such things, and desired to see him. The grammatical commentators note the persistence of his curiosity and its later fruit at Christ’s trial.

The shared, dominant note is the mingling of guilt and idle curiosity in Herod. His ‘John have I beheaded’ betrays the conscience that the rumours stirred; yet his wish to see Jesus was no godly seeking but a craving for a sign or a marvel, the same curiosity that, when at last gratified at the trial, would find Christ silent before him. The witnesses see in Herod a warning against the hearing that is mere curiosity: he desired to see Jesus, but not to obey him, and so his interest, fed by guilt and idleness, led not to faith but to the deeper guilt of mocking the Saviour he had longed to behold. Ellicott notes Luke returns to this desire at Luke 23:8, knowing it from sources unique to him; the witnesses agree Herod’s wish to see Jesus was no godly seeking but a craving for a marvel, the hearing that is mere curiosity, which when at last gratified at the trial would find Christ silent before him. Gill notes Herod had full proof of the beheading, the head brought to him in a charger; the witnesses agree his ‘John have I beheaded’ betrays the conscience the rumours stirred, while his wish to see Jesus was the curiosity that, gratified at last at the trial, would mock the Saviour it had longed to behold.
10:
‘And the apostles, when they were returned, told him all… and he… went aside privately into a desert place… called Bethsaida.’ The witnesses note the apostles’ report and the withdrawal that precedes the feeding.

The fathers read the retreat. Isidore of Pelusium: the Lord, hating men of blood, ‘after the murder of the Baptist left the murderers and departed,’ sparing his enemies and awaiting his own appointed time, not from fear of death. Bede locates Bethsaida by the lake; Ambrose reads the heavenly nourishment given ‘to those who seek Christ in the desert,’ not to the great of the synagogue.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the detail. Robertson observes Bethsaida (Julias) is peculiar to Luke here and lay across the sea (John 6:1); Clarke notes ‘told him all’ (a circumstantial narration). Henry marks the apostles’ faithful report and the retirement for rest.

The shared, dominant note is that the returning apostles told Christ all they had done, and he withdrew with them to a desert place near Bethsaida. The witnesses see in this both the propriety of servants reporting their work to their Master and the Lord’s care to give his wearied disciples rest; and the fathers add a deeper reading — Christ withdrawing from the murderers of John, sparing his foes and waiting his hour, while dispensing the bread of the word to those who sought him in the wilderness. Bede locates Bethsaida by the lake of Gennesaret, the city of Andrew, Peter, and Philip; the witnesses agree the returning apostles told Christ all they had done, and he withdrew with them for rest, the fathers adding that he departed from the murderers of John, sparing his foes and awaiting his hour while dispensing the bread of the word in the wilderness. Ambrose reads the heavenly nourishment given not to the great of the synagogue but to those who seek Christ in the desert; the witnesses agree the apostles’ faithful report to their Master and his care to give them rest set the scene for the feeding, the fathers adding that he withdrew to spare the murderers of John and await his hour.
11:
‘And the people… followed him: and he received them, and spake unto them of the kingdom of God, and healed them that had need of healing.’ The witnesses note Christ’s gracious reception of the pursuing crowd.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the verbs. Robertson notes the imperfects ‘he continued speaking’ and ‘he continued healing’; Ellicott notes the word for ‘healing’ here (used almost only by Luke) is a more technical term, like ‘treatment.’ The Wesleyan Clarke draws out ‘five grand effects of divine grace’: the people are drawn, kindly received, instructed, healed, and fed.

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is on the fruits of repentance, tangential. With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the crowd, learning of his withdrawal, followed, and Christ welcomed them, taught them of the kingdom, and healed the sick among them.

The shared, dominant note is the compassion of Christ, who, seeking rest, yet received the intruding multitude with grace. Far from sending them away, he made their need the occasion of mercy — teaching, healing, and (as the next verses show) feeding them. The witnesses see in this the unwearied goodness of the Saviour, who turned even the interruption of his retreat into a ministry of word and work; and Clarke draws the abiding comfort that Jesus is still the same to all who follow him — receiving, instructing, healing, and feeding the soul that comes to him. Ellicott notes the word for healing here, used almost only by Luke, is a more technical term like ‘treatment’; the witnesses agree Christ, though seeking rest, received the intruding multitude with grace, turning the interruption of his retreat into a ministry of teaching and healing, the unwearied goodness of the Saviour to all who follow him. Clarke draws out five effects of grace — the people drawn, received, instructed, healed, and fed; the witnesses agree the compassion of Christ, who sought rest yet welcomed the intruding crowd, turned even the interruption into a ministry of word and work, and Jesus is still the same to all who follow him.
12:
‘Send the multitude away… for we are here in a desert place.’ The witnesses note the disciples’ counsel and the setting of the feeding.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the scene. Robertson notes ‘to wear away’ (klinein, the sun turning down toward setting), used intransitively only by Luke, and ‘lodge’ (katalysosin, of travellers breaking a journey). Ellicott prefers ‘the villages and farms.’ Barnes refers to the full account (Matthew 14:15).

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Alexandria block here in fact comments on the sending of the Twelve (vv.1–5), a catena offset, and so does not bear on this verse. Wesley refers to the parallels. With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: as the day declined, the Twelve urged Christ to dismiss the crowd to find food and lodging in the surrounding villages, for the place was a wilderness.

The shared, dominant note is the disciples’ well-meant but short-sighted counsel, which sets the stage for the miracle. They saw only the lateness of the hour, the desert place, and the want of provision, and could think of no remedy but to send the people away. The witnesses see in this both the natural reasoning of men at the end of their resources and the contrast with Christ’s purpose: where the disciples would dismiss the hungry, the Lord would feed them, turning their helplessness into the occasion for a display of his creative power. Ellicott prefers ‘the villages and farms’; the witnesses agree the disciples’ well-meant but short-sighted counsel — seeing only the late hour, the desert place, and the want of food — sets the stage for the miracle, the contrast with Christ’s purpose being that where they would dismiss the hungry, the Lord would feed them. Robertson notes the verb ‘to wear away’ (the sun setting) is used intransitively only by Luke; the witnesses agree the disciples saw only the late hour and the want of food, and could think of no remedy but to send the people away, where the Lord purposed to feed them and display his creative power.
13:
‘Give ye them to eat… We have no more but five loaves and two fishes; except we should go and buy meat for all this people.’ The few voices note Christ’s command and the disciples’ scanty store.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the third-class condition ‘except we should go and buy food’ and that ‘food’ (bromata) means ‘eaten pieces.’ Ellicott corrects ‘meat’ to ‘food.’ Gill notes Christ would not send the people away but supply them from the disciples’ own little stock.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ bade the disciples themselves feed the multitude, and they, overwhelmed, pointed to their five loaves and two fishes, supposing only the buying of food could meet so great a need. The grammatical commentators note the conditional form of their bewildered reply.

The shared, dominant note is the testing of the disciples and the exposure of their helplessness. Christ’s ‘give ye them to eat’ laid on them an impossible charge, that they might feel their inability and learn his sufficiency. They could see only the five loaves and the cost of feeding thousands; the Lord saw the occasion for a miracle. The witnesses see in this a pattern of how Christ deals with his servants — setting before them a task beyond their means, that, confessing their emptiness, they may bring their little to him and see it multiplied in his hands to meet the need of the many. Gill notes Christ would not send the people away but supply them from the disciples’ own little stock; the witnesses agree the command ‘give ye them to eat’ laid on them an impossible charge, that they might feel their inability and learn his sufficiency, bringing their little to him to be multiplied in his hands. Robertson notes the third-class condition ‘except we should go and buy food’; the witnesses agree Christ’s charge laid an impossible task on the disciples, that, confessing their emptiness, they might bring their five loaves to him and see them multiplied in his hands to meet the need of the many.
14:
‘For they were about five thousand men. And he said… Make them sit down by fifties in a company.’ The few voices note the number and the orderly arrangement.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the detail. Robertson notes Luke’s ‘about’ (hosei) added to the definite 5,000 men of Mark 6:44, and ‘sit down’ (recline) ‘in companies’ (klisias, table-companies) of about fifty each. Barnes ties the ordering to the rule that all be done decently and in order (1 Corinthians 14:40).

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the men numbered about five thousand, and Christ directed them to recline in ordered groups of fifty. The grammatical commentators note Luke’s approximate number and the distinctive word for the table-companies.

The shared, dominant note is the orderliness of the miracle. Christ did not feed a confused throng but arranged the multitude in ranked companies, that the distribution might be regular and the greatness of the miracle plain to all. The witnesses see in this both a token of the divine love of order — even in a work of creative power, nothing is done in confusion — and a practical wisdom: the seating in fifties made it possible to feed so great a number swiftly and to count them, so that none was overlooked and the wonder of the loaves multiplied could be witnessed and remembered by every company. Barnes ties the ordering to the rule that all be done decently and in order (1 Corinthians 14:40); the witnesses agree Christ arranged the multitude in ranked companies of fifty, that the distribution might be regular, none overlooked, and the greatness of the miracle plain to every company. Robertson notes Luke’s ‘about’ added to the definite five thousand men of Mark 6:44, and the table-companies (klisias) of about fifty; the witnesses agree the orderly seating made it possible to feed so great a number and to witness the wonder in every ranked company, the divine love of order seen even in a work of power.
15:
‘And they did so, and made them all sit down.’ Only one brief voice comments, so the synthesis is short; Gill notes the disciples’ ready obedience.

The Reformed Gill notes the disciples ‘did not dispute the case any longer with Christ, but obeyed his orders,’ ranging the multitude in companies and seating them on the green grass.

With only one brief voice the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. Having objected that they had but five loaves, the disciples now simply obeyed, seating the whole multitude in ordered companies as Christ had directed. Their compliance, where they had just confessed their helplessness, is itself a small act of faith — doing what the Lord commanded though they could not yet see how the people would be fed.

The shared, dominant note, consistent with the surrounding verses, is the obedience that makes way for the miracle. The disciples could not multiply the loaves, but they could arrange the crowd; and in doing the little that lay within their power, at Christ’s word, they prepared the way for the great thing that lay within his. The witnesses see in their ready submission a quiet lesson: where the servant cannot understand, he may still obey, and the obedience of faith, however small the act, is the appointed channel through which the power of Christ is displayed; the orderly seated companies became the very stage on which the feeding was wrought. The witnesses agree the disciples, who had just confessed their helplessness, now simply obeyed, seating the whole multitude as Christ directed; their compliance, doing the little within their power at his word, prepared the way for the great thing within his, the orderly companies becoming the very stage on which the feeding was wrought. Gill notes the disciples disputed no longer but obeyed, seating the people on the green grass; the witnesses agree their ready submission, where they could not understand, was itself a small act of faith, the appointed channel through which the power of Christ would be displayed in the feeding. The witnesses see in their compliance a quiet lesson that where the servant cannot understand he may still obey, and the obedience of faith is the channel of Christ’s power.
16:
‘Then he took the five loaves and the two fishes… he blessed them, and brake, and gave to the disciples to set before the multitude.’ The witnesses note the manner of the miracle.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the verbs. Robertson notes the single aorist ‘brake’ (kateklasen, break thoroughly, only here and the parallel) followed by the picturesque imperfect ‘kept on giving’ (edidou), the loaves multiplying in his hands as he distributed. Clarke draws the minister’s lesson: to take the bread of life himself, lift his soul to God, and rightly divide the word to feed others.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ took the loaves and fishes, looked up to heaven, blessed and broke them, and gave them to the disciples to distribute. The grammatical commentators dwell on the imperfect ‘kept giving’ — the food increasing continually under his hand.

The shared, dominant note is the creative power of Christ joined to thanksgiving and the ordinary means. He looked up and blessed, owning the Father as the giver; he broke and gave through the hands of the disciples, making them the channels of his bounty. The witnesses see in the multiplying loaves a manifest display of divine power — the same Lord who made the world feeding thousands from a few loaves — and a pattern for his ministers, who, taking the bread of life from Christ with prayer, are to break and distribute it to the hungry multitude, themselves but the stewards of a fullness that is his alone. Clarke draws the minister’s lesson: to take the bread of life himself, lift his soul to God, and rightly divide the word to feed others; the witnesses agree the creative power of Christ was joined to thanksgiving and the ordinary means, the loaves multiplying as he broke and gave them through the hands of the disciples, his stewards. Robertson notes the single aorist ‘brake’ followed by the imperfect ‘kept on giving’, the food increasing continually under his hand; the witnesses agree the multiplying loaves were a manifest display of divine power, the same Lord who made the world feeding thousands from a few loaves and a pattern for his ministers, the stewards of a fullness that is his alone.
17:
‘And they did eat, and were all filled: and there was taken up of fragments… twelve baskets.’ The few voices note the abundance of the miracle.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the fullness. Gill notes every one had a part and enough; Barnes ties the filling of the hungry to Psalms 107:9. Robertson and Ellicott note the twelve baskets (kophinoi) of broken pieces (compare Matthew 14:20).

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: all the multitude ate and were satisfied, and twelve baskets of fragments remained. The grammatical commentators dwell on the completeness of the supply — all filled — and the surplus that proved the reality and greatness of the miracle.

The shared, dominant note is the superabundance of Christ’s provision. Not only were the thousands fed to the full from five loaves, but there was more left over than the store with which they began — twelve baskets, one, it may be, for each apostle. The witnesses see in this both an overwhelming proof of the divine power that created bread where none was, and a lesson in the bounty of the Lord, who gives not scantily but to fullness and to spare; the very fragments gathered up testify that he who feeds his people supplies their want with an abundance that exceeds it, wasting nothing yet withholding nothing. Robertson and Ellicott note the twelve baskets (kophinoi) of broken pieces (Matthew 14:20); the witnesses agree the very fragments gathered up testify that he who feeds his people supplies their want with an abundance that exceeds it, wasting nothing yet withholding nothing, giving to fullness and to spare.
18:
‘As he was alone praying… he asked them, saying, Whom say the people that I am?’ The witnesses note Christ at prayer and the question that draws out the great confession.

The fathers mark the prayer and the method. Cyril: the Lord, praying apart, ‘ordained himself an example,’ instructing the rulers of the people to excel in good deeds; and notes ‘the subtle skill of the question,’ leading them first through the opinions of others. Ambrose: it is enough, with Paul, to know nothing ‘but Christ Jesus, and him crucified.’

The grammatical-historical commentators note the detail. Robertson marks the Lukan idiom of prayer (peculiar to Luke that Christ prayed alone here, cf. Luke 3:21); Henry: though Christ had much public work, he found time to be alone in prayer. Ellicott notes Luke omits the walking on the water and the feeding of the four thousand, perhaps not having learnt them. The Catena’s Cyril-of-Alexandria block here comments on the feeding (vv.12–17), a catena offset.

The shared, dominant note is that Christ, praying alone with his disciples near, asked first what the people said of him, to lead them to confess what they themselves believed. The witnesses see in his prayer a mark of Luke’s Gospel, which so often shows Christ at prayer before great moments; and in the question a wise method, drawing the disciples from the varied and inadequate opinions of the crowd toward the true confession that Peter is about to make. Henry notes that though Christ had much public work, he found time to be alone in prayer; the witnesses agree he asked first what the people said of him, to lead the disciples from the varied and inadequate opinions of the crowd toward the true confession that Peter was about to make. Cyril notes the subtle skill of the question, leading the disciples first through the praises and opinions of others; the witnesses agree Christ, praying alone with the disciples near, asked first what the people said of him, to draw them toward the true confession Peter was about to make, Luke ever showing Christ at prayer before great moments.
19:
‘John the Baptist; but some say, Elias; and others say, that one of the old prophets is risen again.’ The few brief voices note the disciples’ report of the popular opinions.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the accusative-and-infinitive in indirect assertion (‘that I am’), where Matthew 16:13 has ‘the Son of man’; the opinions run parallel to the rumours heard by Herod (vv.7–8). Gill notes ‘John the Baptist’ was the view of those who thought him risen from the dead.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the disciples reported the people’s several guesses — John risen, Elijah, an ancient prophet raised. The grammatical commentators note these are the very opinions that had troubled Herod, the highest the people could rise to without confessing the Christ.

The shared, dominant note is the inadequacy of the popular estimate of Jesus. Each opinion granted him a high place — a returned prophet, the forerunner Elijah, the Baptist risen — yet none reached the truth of his person. The witnesses see in this both the impression his works had made and the limit of mere human judgment, which, struck by his greatness, ranked him among the prophets but did not own him the Son of God. The report sets up the contrast of the next verse, where Christ turns from what the people say to what the disciples themselves believe. Robertson notes these are the very opinions that had troubled Herod, the highest the people could rise to without confessing the Christ; the witnesses agree each granted Jesus a high place — a returned prophet, the forerunner Elijah, the Baptist risen — yet none reached the truth of his person. Gill notes ‘John the Baptist’ was the view of those who thought him risen from the dead; the witnesses agree the report set up the contrast of the next verse, where Christ turns from what the people say to what the disciples themselves believe, the popular estimate ranking him among the prophets but not owning him the Son of God.
20:
‘But whom say ye that I am? Peter answering said, The Christ of God.’ The witnesses note Peter’s great confession on behalf of the Twelve.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark its force. Robertson notes the emphatic, proleptic ‘ye’ (hymeis de) — ‘this is really what mattered now’ — and that the confession was not a new discovery but ‘the settled conviction of the disciples’; ‘the Christ of God’ is the Anointed of God, the Messiah. Ellicott notes this precise form is peculiar to Luke, agreeing with ‘the Lord’s Christ’ (Luke 2:26). Barnes refers to the fuller confession in Matthew 16:16.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: to Christ’s pointed question, Peter answered for all, ‘The Christ of God.’ The grammatical commentators stress the emphatic ‘ye’ and the settled faith the confession expresses.

The shared, dominant note is that, against the inadequate guesses of the people, the disciples confess Jesus the Messiah of God. Peter, the spokesman, gives the answer that matters: not a prophet among prophets, but the Christ, the long-promised Anointed. The witnesses see in this the turning-point of the Gospel — the true confession of Christ’s person, drawn out by his question after the people’s opinions had been canvassed; and Luke’s distinctive phrase, ‘the Christ of God,’ sets the seal of divine appointment on the Messiah whom the Twelve, taught of God, now own. Ellicott notes ‘the Christ of God’ is peculiar to Luke, agreeing with ‘the Lord’s Christ’ (Luke 2:26); the witnesses agree Peter, the spokesman, gives the answer that matters — not a prophet among prophets, but the Christ, the long-promised Anointed — the turning-point of the Gospel. Robertson notes the emphatic, proleptic ‘ye’, this being what now mattered, the confession a settled conviction rather than a new discovery; the witnesses agree Peter answered for all, ‘The Christ of God,’ Luke’s distinctive phrase setting the seal of divine appointment on the Messiah whom the Twelve, taught of God, now own. Barnes refers to the fuller confession in Matthew 16:16, where Peter owns him the Son of the living God.
21:
‘And he straitly charged them, and commanded them to tell no man that thing.’ The two brief voices note the charge of silence about his Messiahship.

The grammatical-historical Robertson explains the reason: Jesus ‘had to cease using the word Messiah about himself because of the political meaning to the Jews,’ and its use by the disciples ‘would lead to revolution’ as after the feeding (John 6:15). Gill notes that, though he approved Peter’s confession and pronounced him blessed, he laid this strict charge upon them.

With only two brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. Christ strictly forbade the disciples to publish that he was the Christ. The grammatical commentators trace the prohibition to the danger of the popular, political idea of the Messiah, which a premature proclamation would inflame.

The shared, dominant note is the wisdom of Christ’s reticence. The true confession, good in itself, could not yet be safely published, for the people looked for a political deliverer and would have made him a king by force. Until his death and resurrection had transformed the meaning of ‘Messiah,’ the title was to be kept among the disciples. The witnesses see in the charge both Christ’s avoidance of a worldly kingship and the reason for the prediction that immediately follows: before the Messiahship could be proclaimed, the Son of man must suffer, die, and rise, and so redefine the kingdom the crowds misconceived. Gill notes that, though Christ approved Peter’s confession and pronounced him blessed, he laid this strict charge upon them; the witnesses agree the true confession could not yet be safely published, for the people looked for a political deliverer, and until his death and resurrection the title was to be kept among the disciples. Robertson notes the use of Messiah by the disciples would have led to revolution, as after the feeding (John 6:15); the witnesses agree the charge of silence both avoided a worldly kingship and prepared for the prediction that follows — that before the Messiahship could be proclaimed, the Son of man must suffer, die, and rise.
22:
‘The Son of man must suffer many things… and be slain, and be raised the third day.’ The witnesses note the first plain prediction of the Passion and resurrection.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes ‘rejected’ (apodokimasthenai, rejected after trial), and that ‘the third day’ (Matthew 16:21) and Mark’s ‘after three days’ (Mark 8:31) are the same in sense, not meaning the fourth day. Barnes ties it to the parallels; Wesley: ‘ye must prepare for a scene far different from this.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ foretold, plainly and for the first time, that the Son of man must suffer, be rejected by the rulers, be slain, and rise the third day. The grammatical commentators note the ‘must’ of divine necessity and the harmony of ‘the third day’ with Mark’s phrase.

The shared, dominant note is that, having received the confession of his Messiahship, Christ at once unveils what kind of Messiah he is — a suffering and dying one, who must be rejected and slain. The ‘must’ marks the cross as no accident but the determinate counsel of God. The witnesses see in this both the correction of the disciples’ political hopes — the very reason for the charge of silence — and the joining, even here, of the resurrection to the Passion: the Son of man must die, but on the third day rise, so that the melancholy outlook is set against the sure hope of his rising. Barnes ties it to the parallels, and Wesley bids them prepare for a scene far different from the glory; the witnesses agree Christ, having received the confession, at once unveils what kind of Messiah he is — a suffering and dying one — the ‘must’ marking the cross as the determinate counsel of God, with the resurrection joined to the Passion. Robertson notes ‘the third day’ and Mark’s ‘after three days’ (Mark 8:31) are the same in sense; the witnesses agree the first plain prediction of the Passion corrects the disciples’ political hopes, the ‘must’ marking the cross as no accident but the determinate counsel of God, with the sure hope of the resurrection set against it.
23:
‘If any man will come after me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross daily, and follow me.’ The witnesses note the great call to self-denial and cross-bearing.

The fathers draw it out. Cyril: lest the disciples think Christ alone must suffer while they led a soft life, he shows they too must pass through like struggles. Basil: ‘come after me’ means ‘a due imitation of his life,’ and self-denial is ‘a total forgetfulness of things past, and a forsaking of one’s own will.’ Bede: none comes near to Christ but he who first denies himself.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the words. Robertson observes Luke alone says ‘daily’ (though Clarke notes it is omitted in many manuscripts and not in the parallels, Matthew 16:24, Mark 8:34), and that ‘he said unto all’ (verse 23) shows the lesson was for the multitude as well as the Twelve.

The shared, dominant note is that the way after Christ is the way of self-denial and the cross. To follow him is not to share his glory while shunning his sufferings, but to renounce self, take up the cross, and tread his path. The witnesses see in Luke’s ‘daily’ — whether original or an early gloss, all agree it is true — the abiding, everyday character of the demand: not one great act of sacrifice only, but a continual dying to self; and Wesley draws the lesson that the day is lost wherein no cross is taken up. Wesley draws the lesson that the day is lost wherein no cross is taken up; the witnesses agree the way after Christ is the way of self-denial and the cross, not sharing his glory while shunning his sufferings, but renouncing self and treading his path, Luke’s ‘daily’ marking the everyday character of the demand. Basil reads self-denial as a total forgetfulness of things past and a forsaking of one’s own will; the witnesses agree Luke’s ‘daily,’ whether original or an early gloss, is true in sense, marking not one great act of sacrifice only but a continual dying to self in following Christ.
24:
‘Whosoever will save his life shall lose it: but whosoever will lose his life for my sake, the same shall save it.’ The few voices note the great paradox of losing and saving.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan commentators refer to the fuller treatment of the saying (Matthew 16:25, Mark 8:35, John 12:25). Gill expounds the paradox: he who would save his life by denying Christ loses it eternally, while he who loses it for Christ’s sake saves it. The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is a Passion-meditation, tangential.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the way to save one’s life is to lose it for Christ, and the way to lose it is to save it. The grammatical commentators note the saying’s wide attestation across the Gospels and its place as the ground of the call to self-denial.

The shared, dominant note is the great reversal at the heart of discipleship. To cling to this life and its ease, denying Christ, is to forfeit the true and eternal life; to surrender it for his sake — even unto death — is to secure it. The witnesses see in this the reason for taking up the cross: the loss it seems to entail is the truest gain, and the self-preservation that shrinks from the cross is the truest loss. The paradox, they agree, overturns the world’s reckoning, teaching that only the life laid down for Christ is finally saved. Gill expounds the paradox that he who would save his life by denying Christ loses it eternally; the witnesses agree the loss the cross seems to entail is the truest gain, and the self-preservation that shrinks from it the truest loss, the paradox overturning the world’s reckoning, that only the life laid down for Christ is finally saved. Clarke and Wesley refer to the parallels (Matthew 16:25, John 12:25); the witnesses agree the saying is the ground of the call to self-denial, the loss the cross seems to entail being the truest gain and the self-preservation that shrinks from it the truest loss, so that only the life laid down for Christ is finally saved.
25:
‘For what is a man advantaged, if he gain the whole world, and lose himself, or be cast away?’ The few voices note the worthlessness of the world against the soul.

The Wesleyan Clarke presses the point: if a man received only temporal damage, gaining the world might compensate; ‘but if he should receive spiritual damage… the possession of the universe could not indemnify him.’ Ellicott renders ‘lose himself, or be cast away’ better as ‘destroy himself, or suffer loss,’ the second a softened aspect of the first. Gill notes all the world’s honours and riches are of no service if a man himself is lost.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: to gain the whole world at the cost of oneself is no advantage but utter loss. The grammatical and Wesleyan commentators stress the infinite disproportion — the whole world weighed against the soul, and found worthless.

The shared, dominant note is the supreme value of the soul and the folly of the bargain that loses it. Earthly goods may repair earthly losses, but no gain, not the whole world, can indemnify the man who loses himself. The witnesses see in this the clinching argument for self-denial: since the soul outweighs all the world can offer, it is the height of folly to save the life now and forfeit it for ever, and the truest wisdom to lose all for Christ, who alone can keep the soul that is committed to him against that day. Gill notes all the world’s honours and riches are of no service if a man himself is lost; the witnesses agree the soul outweighs all the world can offer, so that it is the height of folly to save the life now and forfeit it for ever, and the truest wisdom to lose all for Christ, who alone can keep the soul committed to him. Ellicott renders ‘lose himself, or be cast away’ better as ‘destroy himself, or suffer loss’; the witnesses agree earthly goods may repair earthly losses, but no gain, not the whole world, can indemnify the man who loses himself, the value of the soul being the clinching argument for losing all for Christ.
26:
‘Whosoever shall be ashamed of me and of my words, of him shall the Son of man be ashamed, when he shall come in his own glory.’ The witnesses note the warning against shame and the promise of his glorious coming.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes ‘whosoever is ashamed’ (so Mark 8:38), the verb from shame in the eyes of men, while Christ endured the shame of the cross (Hebrews 12:2) and Paul was not ashamed of the gospel (Romans 1:16). Ellicott notes ‘in his own glory’ is peculiar to Luke here, agreeing with John 17:5.

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is on the resurrection (Jonah), tangential. With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: to be ashamed of Christ and his words now is to be disowned by him at his glorious coming.

The shared, dominant note is the solemn reciprocity of confession and shame. As men now, for fear of the world’s scorn, may be ashamed to own Christ and his teaching, so he, coming in his own and his Father’s glory with the holy angels, will be ashamed to own them. The witnesses see in this both a powerful motive to bold confession — the cross-bearing the foregoing verses enjoin — and a warning against the cowardice that shrinks from Christ’s reproach: the shame avoided now is purchased at the price of being disowned then, while the reproach borne for him now is exchanged for his open acknowledgment in glory. Ellicott notes ‘in his own glory’ is peculiar to Luke here, agreeing with John 17:5; the witnesses agree the shame avoided now is purchased at the price of being disowned then, while the reproach borne for Christ now is exchanged for his open acknowledgment when he comes in glory with the holy angels. Robertson notes Christ endured the shame of the cross (Hebrews 12:2) and Paul was not ashamed of the gospel (Romans 1:16); the witnesses agree the cowardice that shrinks from Christ’s reproach now will be disowned by him at his coming, while bold confession is exchanged for his open acknowledgment in glory.
27:
‘There be some standing here, which shall not taste of death, till they see the kingdom of God.’ The witnesses note the promise that some would see the kingdom come.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the idiom ‘till they see’ (with the aorist subjunctive) and the double negative ‘shall not taste of death’ (a rabbinical figure), found in all three (Matthew 16:28, Mark 9:1), Mark adding ‘come with power’; ‘like a physician Christ tasted death that we may see how to die.’ Gill and Barnes take it of the kingdom seen in power. The Catena’s Cyril-of-Alexandria block comments on the self-denial sayings (a catena offset).

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: some then standing would not die before they saw the kingdom of God. The grammatical commentators note the rabbinical figure and the parallels.

The shared, dominant note is that the kingdom would come, in some signal manifestation, within the lifetime of some present. Placed immediately before the Transfiguration, the saying is most naturally read, by many, of that vision of glory soon to be granted the chosen three; others extend it to Pentecost or the fall of Jerusalem. The witnesses agree the coming meant was no distant final advent but a display of the kingdom’s power within that generation — and Luke’s placing it just before the Transfiguration strongly suggests that the ‘some’ who would see it were Peter, James, and John upon the holy mount. Gill and Barnes take it of the kingdom seen in power; the witnesses agree the coming meant was no distant final advent but a display of the kingdom’s power within that generation, and Luke’s placing it just before the Transfiguration strongly suggests the ‘some’ who would see it were Peter, James, and John on the holy mount. Robertson notes the rabbinical figure ‘shall not taste of death’ and that, like a physician, Christ tasted death that we may see how to die; the witnesses agree the coming was a display of the kingdom’s power within that generation, most naturally read of the Transfiguration that immediately follows, though some extend it to Pentecost or the fall of Jerusalem.
28:
‘About an eight days after these sayings, he took Peter and John and James, and went up into a mountain to pray.’ The witnesses note the ascent to the Transfiguration and the reckoning of the days.

The fathers read its purpose. Eusebius: that the disciples might not believe his words only, the Lord proceeds to works, ‘manifesting… the image of his kingdom.’ Damascene harmonises the dates — Matthew and Mark’s ‘six days,’ Luke’s ‘eight’ — as no disagreement. Bede: he ascends to pray and be transfigured, that those who would see the King in glory should have their hearts on high and be ever at prayer; Ambrose reads the three as the human race.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes ‘about eight days’ agrees in general with the ‘after six days’ of Mark 9:2 (Luke counting both end days), and that the mountain was probably Hermon, near Caesarea Philippi; Gill explains the apparent difference the same way. The Tertullian note answers Marcion on Moses and Elijah.

The shared, dominant note is that, about a week after Peter’s confession and the prediction of the cross, Christ took the three chosen apostles up the mountain to pray, and there was transfigured. The witnesses reconcile the ‘eight days’ with the others’ ‘six’ as a difference only of counting, and see in the ascent both the Lukan stress on Christ at prayer and the design of the vision — to confirm the disciples’ faith and brace them, after the announcement of his sufferings, with a glimpse of his coming glory. Bede notes he ascends to pray and be transfigured, that those who would see the King in glory should keep their hearts on high and be ever at prayer; the witnesses reconcile the ‘eight days’ with the others’ ‘six’ as a difference only of counting, and see in the ascent the Lukan stress on Christ at prayer and the design to brace the disciples after the prediction of his cross. Eusebius notes the Lord proceeded from words to works, manifesting the image of his kingdom to the eyes of the disciples’ faith; the witnesses agree that, about a week after Peter’s confession, Christ took the three chosen apostles up the mountain to pray and was transfigured, the vision designed to confirm their faith after the announcement of his sufferings.
29:
‘And as he prayed, the fashion of his countenance was altered, and his raiment was white and glistering.’ The few voices note the transfiguration of Christ as he prayed.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark Luke’s words. Ellicott notes the stress, characteristic of Luke, on Christ’s praying (Luke 3:21), and that Luke, ‘the most classical’ Evangelist, avoids the word ‘transfigured’ (metamorphosed), too suggestive of pagan myth, saying instead ‘the fashion of his countenance was altered.’ Barnes notes the ‘glistering’ whiteness, ‘more white than any fuller could make it’ (Mark 9:3). Gill reads the altered countenance as the answer to his prayer.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: as Christ prayed, his face was changed and his raiment shone with dazzling whiteness. The grammatical commentators dwell on Luke’s noting that the change came ‘as he prayed,’ and on his careful avoidance of the mythological term.

The shared, dominant note is that the Transfiguration came upon Christ in the act of prayer, his inward glory breaking forth in altered face and shining garments. The witnesses see in this both the Lukan emphasis — the glory granted while he prayed, as his earlier prayer at the baptism was answered from heaven — and the nature of the change: not a borrowed splendour but the irradiation of the indwelling Godhead through the veil of the flesh, a foretaste of the glory in which the Son of man will come, given to strengthen the disciples after the prediction of his cross. Barnes notes the ‘glistering’ whiteness, more white than any fuller could make it (Mark 9:3); the witnesses agree the change came ‘as he prayed,’ the inward glory of the indwelling Godhead breaking forth through the veil of the flesh, a foretaste of the glory in which the Son of man will come. Ellicott notes Luke, the most classical Evangelist, avoids the word ‘transfigured’ (metamorphosed) as too suggestive of pagan myth; the witnesses agree the change came as Christ prayed, not a borrowed splendour but the irradiation of the indwelling Godhead through the veil of the flesh, a foretaste of his coming glory.
30:
‘And, behold, there talked with him two men, which were Moses and Elias.’ The few voices note the two great representatives of the Law and the Prophets.

The grammatical-historical Lightfoot cites the Jewish expectation that Moses would come with Elijah, and the rabbinic fancies of Moses raised; he notes Moses represents the Law and Elijah the Prophets, both now in converse with Christ. Gill notes they were ‘two men of great note and fame.’ The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Moses and Elijah appeared and talked with the transfigured Christ. The grammatical commentators note the fitness of the two — the lawgiver and the prophet — doing homage to the Lord whom Law and Prophets foretold.

The shared, dominant note is that the heads of the old covenant attended the glorified Christ. Moses, through whom the Law was given, and Elijah, the chief of the prophets, appeared in glory and spoke with him — a witness that he is no transgressor of the Law nor enemy of the prophets, but their fulfilment and their Lord. The witnesses see in their presence the testimony of the whole Old Testament to Christ, and a fitting prelude to the Father’s voice; for the Law and the Prophets, having borne their witness, will presently give place, that the disciples may hear the beloved Son alone. Lightfoot cites the Jewish expectation that Moses would come with Elijah; the witnesses agree the heads of the old covenant — the lawgiver and the chief of the prophets — appeared in glory to do homage to Christ, a witness that he is no transgressor of the Law but its fulfilment, and a prelude to the Father’s voice bidding the disciples hear the Son alone. Gill notes Moses and Elijah were two men of great note and fame; the witnesses agree the lawgiver and the chief of the prophets, appearing in glory to converse with Christ, bore the testimony of the whole Old Testament to him, a fitting prelude to the Father’s voice that would presently bid the disciples hear the beloved Son alone.
31:
‘Who appeared in glory, and spake of his decease which he should accomplish at Jerusalem.’ The witnesses note the glory of the visitants and the subject of their converse — Christ’s ‘decease.’

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the word. Lightfoot, Barnes, Clarke and Ellicott note ‘decease’ is literally exodus — a ‘departure’ or ‘going out’ — better rendered so, for it covers not his death only but, with an allusion to Israel’s exodus from Egypt, his resurrection and ascension too (compare 2 Peter 1:15); Clarke notes the variant ‘glory’ in some manuscripts, since his death was his glory. This naming of the subject is peculiar to Luke.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Moses and Elijah, appearing in glory, spoke with Christ of the ‘exodus’ he was about to accomplish at Jerusalem. The grammatical commentators dwell on the rich word — a departure that embraces death, rising, and ascension.

The shared, dominant note is that the very glory of the mount pointed to the cross. The theme of the heavenly converse was Christ’s ‘decease’ — the same suffering he had just foretold and Peter had been loth to hear — now spoken of by the Law and the Prophets as the work he should accomplish. The witnesses see in this both the unity of Scripture, Moses and Elijah testifying to the redeeming death, and the deep comfort it brought Christ in view of his Passion; and in the word exodus a hint that his death was a triumphant going-out, like Israel’s from Egypt, by which he would lead his people from bondage to the promised inheritance. Clarke notes the variant ‘glory’ in some manuscripts, since his death was his glory; the witnesses agree the theme of the heavenly converse was Christ’s ‘exodus’ — the very suffering he had just foretold — now spoken of by Moses and Elijah as the work he should accomplish at Jerusalem, the glory of the mount itself pointing to the cross. Lightfoot urges the better rendering ‘departure’ for exodus, since Christ’s ascent into heaven was his going-out as truly as his death; the witnesses agree the word hints that his death was a triumphant exodus, like Israel’s from Egypt, by which he would lead his people from bondage to the promised inheritance.
32:
"But Peter and they that were with him were heavy with sleep: and when they were awake, they saw his glory, and the two men that stood with him." The witnesses note the disciples' drowsiness and the glory they beheld. Theophylact reads the sleep as natural weakness: "Peter is heavy with sleep, for he was weak, and did what was natural to man." Chrysostom offers another sense: "by the word sleep, he means that strange maze that fell upon them by reason of the vision; for it was not night time, but the exceeding brightness of the light weighed down their weak eyes." Ambrose presses the point: "the incomprehensible brightness of the Divine nature oppresses our bodily senses; for if the sight of the body cannot contain the sun's ray, how can the corruption of our fleshly members endure the glory of God?"

The witnesses note that the disciples, once awake, "saw his glory" — the radiance of the transfigured Christ — and the two men, Moses and Elijah, standing with him in glory.

So the witnesses make the verse the disciples' beholding of the Transfiguration glory. The lesson the expositors draw is the weakness of the flesh before the divine glory and the privilege of those who beheld it. The fathers offer two readings of the "heavy sleep," the witnesses note: either the literal drowsiness of weary men (Theophylact), or the daze and overwhelming that the dazzling light produced (Chrysostom, Ambrose) — for the brightness of Christ's transfigured glory was more than mortal eyes could bear, oppressing the senses as the sun overpowers the gaze. Either way, the expositors press, the scene reveals the frailty of human nature in the presence of the divine: the same disciples who would sleep in Gethsemane sleep here, unable to keep pace with their Lord's highest moments. Yet, the witnesses note, grace overcame their weakness: when they awoke, they "saw his glory" — the visible radiance of the Godhead shining through the Saviour's flesh — and Moses and Elijah with him, the law and the prophets attending the Christ in glory. The vision, the expositors observe, was given to strengthen the disciples against the coming offence of the cross, that having seen his glory they might not despair at his passion. The verse thus sets the disciples, roused from their heaviness, as eyewitnesses of the transfigured Lord — a glory their weak frames could scarcely endure, yet which they beheld as a pledge of the majesty that lay behind the lowliness soon to be displayed at Calvary.
33:
"And it came to pass, as they departed from him, Peter said unto Jesus, Master, it is good for us to be here: and let us make three tabernacles; one for thee, and one for Moses, and one for Elias: not knowing what he said." The witnesses note Peter's impulsive proposal and his confusion. The grammatical-historical Robertson observes the timing peculiar to Luke — "as they were departing from him" — so that "this departing of Moses and Elijah apparently accompanied Peter's remark." He notes the varied titles the Gospels preserve: "Master" here, "Rabbi" in Mark, "Lord" in Matthew, and that Luke's "let us make" (where Matthew has "I will make") shows Peter speaking for the three. Barnes cross-references the delight in God's presence — "it is good for us to be here" (Psalm 27:4; 73:28) — and the candid note "not knowing what he said" (cf. Mark 10:38).

The witnesses note Peter's good but mistaken impulse: wishing to detain the glory, to prolong the blessed vision by building shelters for Christ and the two heavenly visitors — a proposal that betrayed, as Luke frankly says, that he "knew not what he said."

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's well-meant but confused response to the glory. The lesson the expositors draw is the natural longing to abide in spiritual blessedness and the imperfection of even a sincere impulse. Peter, the witnesses note, was right in feeling "it is good for us to be here" — the joy of beholding Christ's glory and the company of Moses and Elijah was indeed good, a foretaste of heaven, and the soul rightly delights in God's presence. But his proposal to build three tabernacles, the expositors press, was mistaken: it sought to detain on the mount a glory that was passing, to hold Christ back from the cross that lay before him, and it placed Moses and Elijah, in a sense, alongside Christ — an error the Father's voice would correct by singling out the Son. Luke's candid comment, "not knowing what he said," the witnesses observe, gently exposes the confusion of Peter's mind, overwhelmed by the vision and speaking out of bewilderment rather than understanding. The expositors draw the lesson: even our holiest impulses are often clouded by ignorance, and the desire to linger in spiritual ecstasy may forget the duty that lies below the mount. The verse thus shows Peter, drunk with glory, grasping to prolong it — a sincere but mistaken wish, soon to be answered not by three tabernacles but by the cloud, the voice, and the command to "hear" the beloved Son.
34:
"While he thus spake, there came a cloud, and overshadowed them: and they feared as they entered into the cloud." The witnesses note the cloud of the divine presence and the disciples' fear. Chrysostom (on v.32) had read the cloud as a token of Christ's equality with the Father: "as in the Old Testament the Lord dwelt in the cloud, so now also a cloud received our Lord — not a dark cloud, but bright and shining." The grammatical-historical Robertson notes Luke's inchoative imperfect "overshadowed" — "the shadow began to come upon them," as a cloud swiftly covers a high mountain's cap — and that the same verb is used "of the Holy Spirit upon Mary" (Luke 1:35). He notes "all six entered into the cloud, but only Peter, James, and John became afraid." Wesley reads it: the apostles "feared, while they (Moses and Elijah) entered into the cloud, which took them away."

The witnesses note the awe the cloud inspired — the symbol of God's near presence, as of old over the tabernacle — and the holy fear that seized the disciples as it enveloped them.

So the witnesses make the verse the descent of the cloud of glory. The lesson the expositors draw is the manifestation of the divine presence and the awe it rightly produces. The cloud, the witnesses note, was no ordinary mist but the symbol of God's presence — the Shekinah that had dwelt over the tabernacle and filled the temple, now overshadowing the transfigured Christ and his companions. Chrysostom's observation, the expositors press, finds in it a witness to Christ's deity: as the Lord dwelt in the cloud in the old covenant, so the cloud now received the Son, marking him not inferior to the Father but sharing the divine glory — and it was a bright cloud, the cloud of glory, not the dark cloud of Sinai's terror. The witnesses note Robertson's link to the annunciation: the same word for "overshadow" describes the Spirit's coming upon Mary (Luke 1:35), joining the mystery of the incarnation to the glory of the Transfiguration. And the fear of the disciples, the expositors observe, was the proper response of the creature to the near presence of God — a holy awe, such as fell on Israel at Sinai, as the cloud enveloped them. The verse thus brings the divine presence visibly down upon the mount, preparing for the voice that would speak from the cloud; and it sets the trembling disciples within the very glory of God, that they might hear, with reverent fear, the Father's testimony to his Son.
35:
"And there came a voice out of the cloud, saying, This is my beloved Son: hear him." The witnesses note the Father's testimony and a textual question over the title. The grammatical-historical Robertson identifies the voice: "the voice of the Father, like that at the baptism of Jesus (Luke 3:22) and like that near the end (John 12:28-30)." On the title, Ellicott and Clarke note the better manuscripts read "my chosen" (ho eklelegmenos) rather than "beloved" — Ellicott judging "beloved" likely "originated in the wish to produce a verbal agreement with the other Gospels," and comparing the like word in 1 Peter 2:4, 6. Barnes anchors "hear him" in Moses' prophecy of the prophet like himself, to whom all must hearken (Deuteronomy 18:15; Acts 3:22).

The Father's word, the witnesses note, both declares Christ's unique Sonship and commands obedience to him — "hear him" — setting the Son above Moses and Elijah, the law and the prophets, who must now yield to the One they foretold.

So the witnesses make the verse the climax of the Transfiguration: the Father's own testimony to the Son. The lesson the expositors draw is the supreme authority of Christ, attested by the Father and to be heard above all. The voice from the cloud, the witnesses note, is the Father's — the same that spoke at the baptism, bearing witness to the beloved (or chosen) Son; and here, on the mount of glory, it answers Peter's confused proposal to honour Moses and Elijah equally with Christ, by singling out the Son alone: "This is my beloved Son." The textual variant, the expositors observe, scarcely alters the sense — whether "beloved" or "chosen," the title marks Christ as the one in whom the Father delights, the elect of God, the unique Son. But the decisive word, the witnesses press, is the command "hear him." Drawn from Moses' own prophecy of the coming Prophet whom Israel must obey (Deuteronomy 18:15), it sets Christ above the law and the prophets: Moses and Elijah, present in glory, are heard no longer; the Son alone is now to be heard. The very presence of the great representatives of the old covenant, yielding to the Son at the Father's word, the expositors note, declares that the age of the law and the prophets has given way to the age of Christ. The verse thus crowns the vision with the Father's testimony and command — the Son is divine, beloved, chosen, and supreme; let all men hear him.
36:
"And when the voice was past, Jesus was found alone. And they kept it close, and told no man in those days any of those things which they had seen." The witnesses note the vanishing of the heavenly visitors and the disciples' silence. The grammatical-historical Gill explains: when the voice "was delivered and heard no more, Jesus was found alone by his disciples; Moses and Elias being gone, and he in the same form as before his transfiguration." Barnes notes "the two men had left him; in respect to them he was alone." On the silence, the witnesses note the disciples "told no man in those days" — keeping the vision secret, as Christ charged them, until after his resurrection.

The vision past, the witnesses note, only Jesus remained — the glory withdrawn, Moses and Elijah gone, the Son alone, as the Father's "hear him" had set him alone; and the disciples kept the wonder to themselves.

So the witnesses make the verse the close of the Transfiguration. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, the significance of "Jesus found alone": when the voice had spoken and the cloud passed, Moses and Elijah were gone, and Christ stood alone — a fitting seal upon the Father's word. The law and the prophets, represented by the two, had borne their witness and withdrawn; the Son alone remained, the one to be heard. The witnesses note the quiet theology of it: all that the old covenant pointed to is gathered up and fulfilled in Christ, who is left alone as the sole object of faith and obedience. Second, the disciples' silence: they "told no man in those days" what they had seen — keeping the vision close, in obedience (as the other Gospels record) to Christ's charge, until his resurrection should have come. The expositors note the wisdom of this reserve: the glory of the Transfiguration, told too soon, would have ill-accorded with the lowliness and the cross that lay before Christ, and might have confirmed the disciples' and the people's false hopes of a triumphant Messiah; but after the resurrection, the vision would stand as a confirmation of his glory. The verse thus ends the scene with the Son alone and the witnesses silent — the glory withdrawn into the ordinary, the disciples treasuring in secret the pledge of the majesty they had glimpsed, against the dark day of the passion now drawing near.
37:
"And it came to pass, that on the next day, when they were come down from the hill, much people met him." The witnesses note the descent from the mount to the need below. Bede draws the contrast: "certain places accord with certain events. On the Mount our Lord prays, is transfigured, reveals the secrets of His glory; as He descends to the lower parts, He is received by a large concourse. Above He makes known the voice of the Father, below He expels the evil spirits." Titus of Bostra commends the father's wise plea (to come): he "said not, Do this or that, but, Look on my son, for this suffices for His salvation; as the prophet said, Look on me, and have mercy on me."

The witnesses note the swift transition from the glory of the mount to the misery of the valley — the transfigured Lord descending the next day to meet a needy crowd and a demoniac boy.

So the witnesses make the verse the descent from glory to ministry. The lesson the expositors draw is the contrast between the mountain of vision and the valley of need, and Christ's readiness to serve in both. Bede's observation, the witnesses note, is striking: on the mount, Christ prayed, was transfigured, and heard the Father's voice; descending, he was met by a thronging crowd and a father's anguished plea, and there cast out the evil spirit. The glory above and the warfare below, the expositors press, belong together in the Saviour's work: he does not linger on the mount (as Peter wished) but comes down to the suffering world, bringing the power of his glory to bear on human misery. The witnesses note the lesson for disciples: spiritual heights are not given for their own sake nor to be clung to, but to equip the soul for service in the valley; the vision of glory is followed by the casting out of devils.
