The verse thus turns from the Transfiguration to the healing of the epileptic boy, joining the two scenes the Gospels place side by side — the mount of glory and the valley of demonic affliction — and showing the transfigured Christ at once Lord of glory and conqueror of the powers of darkness, who comes down from the heights to meet the deepest human need.
38:
"And, behold, a man of the company cried out, saying, Master, I beseech thee, look upon my son: for he is mine only child." The witnesses note the father's anguished plea and Luke's characteristic tenderness. The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes "a man from among the multitude" used the word "teacher," and notes the tender touch: "he is mine only child — note, as in the history of the widow of Nain (Luke 7:12), the tender sympathy shown in St. Luke's narrative; he is the only Evangelist who calls attention to the fact." Robertson notes the verb "to look upon" (epiblepsai) is "common in medical writers for examining carefully the patient," and that "only child" (monogenēs) appears in Luke for the widow's son and Jairus's daughter (Luke 7:12; 8:42).

The father, the witnesses note, asks not for a particular act but simply that Christ "look upon" his son — a humble, trusting plea — and presses his grief by noting the boy is his "only child."

So the witnesses make the verse the father's tender appeal. The lesson the expositors draw is the pathos of the plea and the physician-evangelist's eye for sympathy. The father, the witnesses note, came out of the crowd with an urgent cry — "Master, I beseech thee, look upon my son" — asking, in his humility, only that Christ would regard his child, trusting that the Lord's mere look would suffice. Titus of Bostra (on v.37) had commended the wisdom of this: he did not presume to prescribe the cure but left it to Christ, like the psalmist's "Look on me, and have mercy." And the detail "he is mine only child," the expositors press, is characteristic of Luke: the physician-evangelist, with his tender eye for human suffering, alone records that the boy was an only son — as he alone notes the only son of the widow of Nain and the only daughter of Jairus. The witnesses note Robertson's observation that even the verb "look upon" is a medical word for careful examination, fitting the doctor's pen. The detail deepens the appeal: the father's whole hope rested on this one child, his only consolation, now grievously afflicted. The verse thus opens the account of the epileptic boy with a father's trusting, grief-laden plea, and displays the tender sympathy that marks Luke's Gospel — the only child, the anguished parent, the humble request that Christ would but look upon his suffering son.
39:
"And, lo, a spirit taketh him, and he suddenly crieth out; and it teareth him that he foameth again, and bruising him hardly departeth from him." The witnesses note the violence of the demonic affliction. The grammatical-historical Gill identifies it as "an evil spirit, the devil," who "seizes and possesses him at once." Adam Clarke supplies a striking parallel from Herodotus: "this very phrase [a spirit taketh him] is used by heathen writers when they speak of supernatural influence," citing the Scythians who said of their king under Bacchic frenzy, "the God possesses us... the same demon has taken possession of your king" — illustrating that the language of demonic seizure was widely understood in the ancient world.

The witnesses note the dreadful symptoms — sudden crying out, convulsions, foaming, bruising — which the Gospels describe in terms resembling epilepsy, yet trace to a real demonic possession that the spirit "hardly" (with difficulty) left.

So the witnesses make the verse a description of the boy's terrible bondage. The lesson the expositors draw is the reality and cruelty of demonic power over its victims. The father, the witnesses note, details the affliction: a spirit seizes the boy, who suddenly cries out, is torn with convulsions, foams at the mouth, and is bruised — a torment from which the spirit departs only with difficulty, leaving the child shattered. The Gospels' description, the expositors observe, resembles the symptoms of epilepsy (the "lunatic" of Matthew 17:15), yet Luke and the others trace the cause not to mere bodily disease but to a real evil spirit — the physician-evangelist himself, careful to distinguish demonic possession from natural illness, presenting this as a case of genuine possession. Clarke's parallel from Herodotus, the witnesses note, shows that the ancient world commonly spoke of supernatural beings "taking" or "possessing" men, the very language the father uses; the phenomenon was recognized, however differently explained. The expositors press the cruelty of Satan's tyranny: the evil spirit delights to torment, casting the boy into fire and water, convulsing and bruising him, sparing not even a child. The verse thus sets forth the misery from which Christ would deliver the boy — a real and violent demonic bondage, beyond all human help — and prepares for the contrast between the disciples' failure and the easy victory of the Lord over the powers of darkness.
40:
"And I besought thy disciples to cast him out; and they could not." The witnesses note the disciples' failure and its significance. The grammatical-historical Gill identifies them as "the nine disciples that were left behind, whilst Christ and the other three were gone up to the mountain." Barnes cross-references a like failure of those who presumed to use Christ's name without his power (the sons of Sceva, Acts 19:13-16). The disciples, the witnesses note, had been given power over unclean spirits (Luke 9:1), yet here they failed — a failure Christ would attribute (in the parallel accounts) to their want of faith and prayer.

The father's complaint, the witnesses note, is not an accusation but a statement of his extremity: he had sought help from the disciples, and they could not give it, so that his last hope lay in Christ himself.

So the witnesses make the verse the record of the disciples' impotence. The lesson the expositors draw is the insufficiency of even the commissioned disciples apart from faith and dependence on Christ. The nine, the witnesses note, had been left below while the three went up the mount; and when the desperate father brought his son, they attempted the cure — for they had been given authority over demons — but "they could not." The expositors press the cause the Gospels supply: it was their unbelief and prayerlessness (Matthew 17:20-21; Mark 9:29) — a failure of faith, perhaps a presumptuous reliance on the power once given rather than on living dependence on Christ. The witnesses note Barnes's pointed cross-reference: the sons of Sceva, who used Christ's name without his power, were overcome by the evil spirit; so the disciples' failure warns against trusting in office or past gifts apart from present faith. The expositors observe the providential purpose: their failure set off, by contrast, the easy victory of Christ, magnifying his power where theirs fell short; and it taught them their continual need of him. The father's report, the witnesses note, is the plea of a man at the end of his resources — human help having failed, even the help of Christ's own disciples — casting him wholly upon the Lord. The verse thus exposes the disciples' weakness and prepares for Christ's lament over the faithless generation and his triumphant deliverance of the boy.
41:
"And Jesus answering said, O faithless and perverse generation, how long shall I be with you, and suffer you? Bring thy son hither." The witnesses note Christ's lament and its true object. The Reformed Gill argues the rebuke falls not on the disciples but on the unbelieving Jews: the words "are not spoken to the disciples, as they might seem... but to the unbelieving Jews," and "with a particular view to the Scribes and Pharisees, who had been insulting the disciples" over their failure. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes Luke combines Mark's two questions into one — "how long shall I be with you and bear with you?" — and glosses "faithless" (disbelieving) and "perverse" (diestrammenē, "twisted, turned, or torn in two"). Barnes anchors the terms in Moses' song of a "perverse and crooked generation" (Deuteronomy 32:5; Psalm 78:8).

Christ's lament, the witnesses note, expresses the grief and holy weariness of the Lord at the persistent unbelief surrounding him — yet it ends in mercy: "Bring thy son hither."

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's sorrowful rebuke of unbelief. The lesson the expositors draw is the grief of Christ over a faithless generation and his readiness, nonetheless, to show mercy. The witnesses note the debate over whom Christ addresses: Gill holds the rebuke aimed chiefly at the unbelieving Jews and the gloating scribes, not the disciples, whose fault was lesser; others extend it to the disciples' want of faith as well. But all agree the lament is over unbelief — "O faithless and perverse generation" — the disbelieving, twisted, crooked spirit that surrounded Christ on every side, in the failed disciples, the doubting father, and above all the hostile crowd. The expositors note the weight of Christ's words: "how long shall I be with you, and suffer you?" — the cry of holy weariness, the Lord grieved at the slowness and perversity of those among whom he laboured, and longing for the faith he so rarely found. Barnes's link to Moses' song, the witnesses observe, sets this generation in the line of Israel's recurring unbelief, a "perverse and crooked generation." Yet, the expositors press, the lament does not end in rejection but in mercy: "Bring thy son hither" — Christ, grieved as he is by unbelief, still receives the afflicted and delivers him. The verse thus joins the Saviour's sorrow over a faithless world with his unfailing compassion for the suffering, rebuking the unbelief that hindered the cure even as he moves to grant it.
42:
"And as he was yet a coming, the devil threw him down, and tare him. And Jesus rebuked the unclean spirit, and healed the child, and delivered him again to his father." The witnesses note the demon's last assault, the swift cure, and the tender restoration. The grammatical-historical Robertson describes the violence: "the devil threw him down (dashed him, a verb used of boxers' knockout blows) and tare him grievously," but Christ "rebuked the unclean spirit, and healed the child." He notes the tender close, peculiar to Luke: "gave him back to his father — a tender touch alone in Luke (as in 7:15)," and that "they were all astonished... a picturesque description of the amazement of all at the easy victory of Jesus where the nine disciples had failed."

The witnesses note the contrast: the demon, even in departing, convulsed the boy with one last violence; but Christ, with a word of rebuke, healed him at once and gave him back to his father.

So the witnesses make the verse the deliverance of the boy. The lesson the expositors draw is the easy and complete victory of Christ over the powers of darkness, and his tender mercy. Even as the boy was being brought, the witnesses note, the demon made a final, furious assault — throwing him down and convulsing him, as Satan rages most when about to lose his prey. But Christ needed only to "rebuke the unclean spirit": with a word he overcame the power that the nine disciples could not, healed the child, and cast out the demon. The expositors press the contrast Robertson draws: the disciples had labored and failed; Christ conquered with a single command, so that all were astonished at the majesty of God displayed in so easy a triumph. The deliverance, the witnesses note, magnified the power of the Lord precisely where his servants' weakness had been exposed — the failure of the nine setting off the omnipotence of the Master. And the tender close, the expositors observe, is characteristic of Luke: Christ "delivered him again to his father" — not merely casting out the demon but restoring the healed son to the anxious parent, as he had given the widow of Nain's son back to his mother (Luke 7:15). The verse thus completes the scene with a double note: the sovereign power of Christ over Satan, conquering with a word, and his tender compassion, restoring the only child to his father's arms — the glory of the mount now manifest in the casting out of the devil in the valley.
43:
"And they were all amazed at the mighty power of God. But while they wondered every one at all things which Jesus did, he said unto his disciples..." The witnesses note the crowd's astonishment and Christ's turning from praise to the prediction of his passion. Cyril draws the contrast: "although all men marveled at those things which He did, He addresses what follows not to all, but to His disciples"; for the disciples might be perplexed at his coming passion — "Have we then been deceived in that we thought him to be God?" — and so Christ bids them "lay up in their minds the mystery of His passion." Titus of Bostra presses the point: "while all were wondering at the miracles, He foretells His passion; for miracles do not save, but the cross conveys the benefit."

The witnesses note the deliberate timing: at the height of popular wonder, when all marveled at his power, Christ turns aside to warn his disciples of his coming suffering — checking their elation with the shadow of the cross.

So the witnesses make the verse the hinge between the miracle and the passion prediction. The lesson the expositors draw is the wisdom of Christ in tempering glory with the cross. The crowd, the witnesses note, was "amazed at the mighty power of God" displayed in the boy's deliverance, wondering at all Christ did; and it was precisely at this moment of popular acclaim that Christ turned to his disciples with the word of his coming suffering. The fathers find deep purpose in this, the expositors press: the disciples, swept up in the wonder of his miracles and the people's praise, were in danger of fixing their hopes on a triumphant, glorious Messiah, and would be shattered when he was delivered to death. So Christ, at the very height of his glory, foretells his passion — that they might be forewarned, and not stumble when it came, nor doubt his deity, but understand that the cross, not the miracles, was the heart of his saving work. Titus's word, the witnesses observe, is weighty: "miracles do not save, but the cross conveys the benefit" — the wonders attest the Saviour, but it is his death that redeems. The expositors note Cyril's pastoral insight: Christ addresses the warning to the disciples alone, not the crowd, who needed first to be confirmed in his power; the deeper mystery of the passion was for those nearest him. The verse thus turns from the marveling multitude to the sober word of the cross, joining the glory of Christ's power to the necessity of his suffering.
44:
"Let these sayings sink down into your ears: for the Son of man shall be delivered into the hands of men." The witnesses note Christ's solemn charge and the second passion prediction. The Reformed Gill explains the charge: Christ would have the disciples "diligently attend, seriously consider, and carefully lay up in their memories" what he was about to say — "not the words he had spoken, but what he was about to say." Some versions read "into your hearts." Cyril (on v.43) had read the aim: that they "lay up in their minds as a certain deposit the mystery of His passion," distinguished by the word "your" from the crowd, who were not yet to know he would suffer. Barnes offers an alternative — that "these sayings" may refer to the people's praise and the evidence of his miracles, to be remembered when he died.

The prediction itself, the witnesses note, is plain: "the Son of man shall be delivered into the hands of men" — a foretelling of his betrayal and passion, set against the very wonder of his power.

So the witnesses make the verse the second foretelling of the passion and the charge to remember it. The lesson the expositors draw is the importance Christ placed on his disciples' grasping the necessity of his suffering. He prefaces the prediction with a solemn appeal — "let these sayings sink down into your ears" — that they might not let the coming word slip, but lodge it deep in mind and heart, against the day of its fulfilment. The witnesses note the debate over "these sayings": Gill takes them to be the passion-prediction itself, which they were to fix in memory; Barnes suggests they may include the recent miracles and the people's confession, to be recalled as proof of his Messiahship when he died. Either way, the expositors press, the purpose was the same — to prepare the disciples for the cross, that when the Son of man was "delivered into the hands of men," they might not be wholly overthrown, having been forewarned. Cyril's note, the witnesses observe, marks the privacy: this deep mystery was for the disciples, not the crowd, who needed first to be confirmed in his power before they could bear the word of his suffering. The verse thus joins the solemn charge to the plain prediction: Christ, at the height of his glory, presses upon his own the truth they were so slow to receive — that he must be betrayed and suffer — bidding them treasure it deep, that it might steady their faith in the dark hour to come.
45:
"But they understood not this saying, and it was hid from them, that they perceived it not: and they feared to ask him of that saying." The witnesses note the disciples' incomprehension and its cause. The grammatical-historical Gill explains: "they knew not how these things could possibly be, for they could not reconcile them with the notions they had of a temporal Redeemer and victorious Messiah." Barnes presses the cause: "it was hid from them — not by God, but by their previous false belief; they had imbibed the common notions of the Jews that he was to be a prince and conqueror... and from this we may learn that the plainest truths are unintelligible to many because they have embraced some erroneous opinion before, which they are unwilling to abandon."

The witnesses note the threefold note of their failure: they "understood not," it was "hid from them," and they "feared to ask" — a mixture of intellectual blindness and reluctance to face the dreadful truth.

So the witnesses make the verse a study in the disciples' blindness to the cross. The lesson the expositors draw is how preconceived error blinds men to the plainest truth. The saying was clear enough — the Son of man would be delivered to death; yet the disciples "understood not," for the prediction clashed utterly with their fixed expectation of a triumphant, reigning Messiah who would deliver Israel and reign in glory. The witnesses note Barnes's important distinction: the truth was "hid from them" not by any act of God concealing it, but by their own false preconceptions, which made them unable to receive a meaning so contrary to their hopes. The expositors draw the abiding lesson: men are blinded to the clearest teaching of Scripture when they cling to opinions formed beforehand; the proper way is to lay aside all prior notions and submit wholly to God's word. The disciples should have suspected their own ideas of the Messiah were wrong and bowed to Christ's plain word; instead, the saying remained dark to them. And they "feared to ask," the witnesses note — partly perhaps from reverence, partly from a reluctance to have confirmed a truth so unwelcome, the dread of the cross making them shrink from understanding. The verse thus exposes the persistent blindness even of Christ's own, who, fixed on a Messiah of glory, could not take in the word of his suffering; and it warns the reader against the prejudice that hides the truth from the prejudiced.
46:
"Then there arose a reasoning among them, which of them should be greatest." The witnesses note the disciples' ambition and its causes. Cyril reads it as Satan's subtler snare: "the devil lays plots of various kinds for them that love the best way of life... if a man has escaped carnal allurements, he excites in him a desire of glory; and this passion for vain-glory had seized some one of His apostles." He suggests not all gave way, the evangelist speaking indefinitely "not to lay the charge to any individual." Theophylact traces the occasion to the failed exorcism: "this feeling was excited by their not being able to cure the demoniac... one said, It was not owing to my weakness, but another's." Bede suggests another cause: because Peter, James, and John were "taken apart to the mount, and the keys promised to Peter, they were angry that these three should be preferred."

The witnesses note the irony: immediately after Christ foretold his humiliation, the disciples disputed about their own greatness — the spirit of the world intruding upon the threshold of the cross.

So the witnesses make the verse the occasion of Christ's lesson on humility. The lesson the expositors draw is the persistence of pride even in the disciples, and the subtlety of the temptation to vain-glory. The witnesses note the painful contrast: Christ had just spoken of being delivered to death, and the disciples fell to disputing "which of them should be greatest" — so far were they from grasping the way of the cross. The fathers probe the causes, the expositors observe: Cyril sees the devil's craft, who, failing to ensnare the godly by fleshly lusts, attacks them through ambition and the love of glory — a subtler and more dangerous snare for those who have renounced grosser sins. Theophylact ties the strife to the recent failure: the nine, unable to cast out the demon, fell to blaming one another and so to comparing their standing. Bede suggests jealousy of the three honoured on the mount and of Peter's promised keys. The witnesses note Cyril's charitable point: the evangelist names no individual, and perhaps not all the disciples shared the fault. But the lesson stands, the expositors press: the love of preeminence is a deep-rooted sin, intruding even among Christ's chosen, even at the most solemn moments — and it must be met, as Christ will meet it, by the example of the child and the law of the kingdom, that the least is the greatest. The verse thus opens the teaching on humility, exposing the ambition that lurked in the apostles' hearts.
47:
"And Jesus, perceiving the thought of their heart, took a child, and set him by him." The witnesses note Christ's discernment and the object-lesson. The Reformed Gill marks the omniscience: "not by any words he had heard, for the dispute was on the road... but as the omniscient God, the discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart, he was privy to all their ambition." The grammatical-historical Robertson describes the scene: "taking a little child to himself... set him by his side (Mark has 'in his arms,' Matthew 'in the midst')" — "the little child (Peter's child?) was slipped down by the side of Jesus as he gave the disciples an object lesson in humility which they sorely needed."

Christ, the witnesses note, read the disputing hearts the disciples had not voiced aloud, and answered not with reproof only but with a living parable — a child set beside him as the model of greatness in the kingdom.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's gentle but searching response to the disciples' ambition. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold: Christ's knowledge of the heart, and his method of teaching humility by a child. The witnesses note, with Gill, that the dispute had been carried on privately, on the road, out of Christ's hearing as men reckon; yet he "perceived the thought of their heart" — for he is the omniscient God who searches the secret reasonings of men, knowing their ambition though no word reached his ear. This discernment, the expositors press, was itself a rebuke: the disciples who disputed about greatness stood before One who read their proud hearts. And Christ's response, the witnesses note, was not a bare reproof but a vivid object-lesson: he took a little child — small, lowly, unambitious, dependent — and set him by his side, that the disciples might see in the child the very pattern of the greatness they sought amiss. The expositors observe the tenderness of the method: rather than shame them with words alone, Christ teaches by a living emblem, placing the child where all could see, and making the least and humblest the measure of true greatness. The verse thus joins the divine knowledge of the heart to the gracious wisdom of the Teacher: Christ exposes the disciples' ambition by reading their thoughts, and corrects it by the child at his side — preparing for the saying that the least among them is the greatest.
48:
"And said unto them, Whosoever shall receive this child in my name receiveth me; and whosoever shall receive me receiveth him that sent me: for he that is least among you all, the same shall be great." The witnesses expound the paradox of greatness. The Reformed Gill explains "this child" as "one such little child... a child like to this, not in age, but in meekness and humility; one that is not proud, ambitious, and envious." The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the better text reads "the same is great" — "the greatness not thrown forward as a future compensation, but actually attained in the midst of, and by means of, the seeming humiliation." Barnes cross-references the law of the kingdom: the humble exalted (Matthew 23:11-12; Luke 14:11).

The witnesses note the double teaching: to receive the lowly (the childlike believer, or the humble themselves) is to receive Christ and the Father; and the truly great in the kingdom is the one who is least, lowliest in his own eyes.

So the witnesses make the verse the law of greatness in Christ's kingdom. The lesson the expositors draw is the complete reversal of the world's measure of greatness. Christ answers the disciples' dispute, the witnesses note, with a twofold word. First, the dignity of the lowly: "whosoever shall receive this child in my name receiveth me" — to welcome the humble believer, represented by the child, is to welcome Christ himself, and through him the Father who sent him; so that honour shown to the least of Christ's people is honour shown to God. The expositors note Gill's point that the "child" stands for the childlike Christian, marked not by youth but by meekness, humility, and freedom from ambition — the very opposite of the disciples' strife. Second, and decisively, the principle of true greatness: "he that is least among you all, the same shall be great." The witnesses note Ellicott's textual observation, that the better reading is "is great" — the greatness not a future reward only but a present reality, attained precisely in and through the humility the world despises. The lowliest, the expositors press, is the greatest in the kingdom of God; the way up is down; the path to true honour is the renouncing of ambition and the embracing of lowly service. The verse thus overturns the disciples' whole notion of greatness: not he who exalts himself, but he who is least, is great in Christ's kingdom — and to receive such lowly ones is to receive Christ and the Father.
49:
"And John answered and said, Master, we saw one casting out devils in thy name; and we forbad him, because he followeth not with us." The witnesses note John's report of the strange exorcist and the spirit behind it. Cyril of Alexandria frames the section as teaching on receiving Christ's true ministers. Gill and the witnesses note that John, perhaps stirred by Christ's words about receiving in his name, recalls a man who cast out demons in that name yet did not belong to the apostolic company, whom the disciples had forbidden. The connection, the witnesses observe, is the phrase "in thy name": John wonders whether the disciples were right to forbid one who used Christ's name but "followeth not with us."

The disciples' act, the witnesses note, sprang from a sectarian zeal — a desire to restrict the work of Christ to their own circle, forbidding one who did good but was not of their number.

So the witnesses make the verse the raising of the question of toleration. The lesson the expositors draw is the narrowness of a sectarian spirit, even in the apostles. John, the witnesses note, perhaps prompted by Christ's saying about receiving men "in my name," brings up a case that troubled him: they had found a man casting out devils in Christ's name — doing the very work Christ did, and successfully — yet they had forbidden him, "because he followeth not with us." The motive, the expositors press, was a kind of jealousy for their own company: the man was not of the Twelve, did not follow with them, and so they presumed to silence him, as though the power and the work of Christ belonged to their circle alone. The witnesses note the irony, coming hard upon the dispute about greatness: the same ambition that made them strive for preeminence made them intolerant of others' service. Yet John's mention of it, the expositors observe, has something honest and teachable in it — he lays the matter before Christ, half-doubting whether they had done rightly. The verse thus sets up Christ's rebuke of the sectarian spirit: the disciples had forbidden a man for not belonging to their party, though he wrought good in Christ's name; and Christ will answer, in the next verse, with the great word of toleration — "forbid him not" — teaching that the work of God is not confined to one company, and that he who is not against Christ is for him.
50:
"And Jesus said unto him, Forbid him not: for he that is not against us is for us." The witnesses note Christ's rebuke of intolerance and the proverb. The grammatical-historical Robertson examines the saying: Mark has "against us is for us," and "the Greek e and u were often pronounced alike," so manuscripts vary between "us" and "you"; the point is "precisely the same, a proverbial saying." He compares the converse word, "He that is not with me is against me" (Luke 11:23), and notes the prohibition is general — "Stop hindering him" (present imperative). Robertson presses the application: "the lesson of toleration in methods of work for Christ is needed today." Barnes cross-references the same principle (Matthew 12:30).

The witnesses note Christ overturns the disciples' narrow exclusiveness: the man who does good in Christ's name, though not of their company, is not to be forbidden but welcomed as on Christ's side.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's lesson of toleration. The lesson the expositors draw is the breadth of Christ's cause and the rebuke of a sectarian spirit. The witnesses note the directness of Christ's "Forbid him not" — a general prohibition, "stop hindering him," correcting the disciples' impulse to silence one who was not of their number. The ground is the proverb: "he that is not against us is for us." The expositors press its meaning: a man who casts out devils in Christ's name, who does the work of the kingdom and honours that name, is plainly not an enemy of Christ; and not being against him, he is to be reckoned for him, however he stands related to the apostolic company. The witnesses note Robertson's pairing with the converse saying (Luke 11:23, "he that is not with me is against me"): the two are not contradictory but apply to different cases — the one to those who actively oppose Christ (no neutrality is possible toward him), the other to fellow-workers who serve him by other means (such are not to be hindered). The expositors draw the abiding lesson, with Robertson, that the Church needs this word still: the work of Christ is wider than any one party, and those who labour for him in ways not our own are not to be forbidden but owned as allies. The verse thus closes the section with Christ's gracious breadth: against the narrow zeal that would confine his cause to its own circle, he declares that all who are not against him are for him, and bids his disciples cease to hinder the servants of the same Lord.
51:
"And it came to pass, when the time was come that he should be received up, he stedfastly set his face to go to Jerusalem." The witnesses note the great turning-point of Luke's Gospel and Christ's resolve. Cyril marks the time: "when the time was near in which it behoved our Lord to accomplish His life-giving Passion, and ascend to heaven, He determines to go up to Jerusalem." Titus of Bostra notes the necessity: "the true Lamb should be offered where the typical lamb was sacrificed"; and "he stedfastly set his face" means "He went not here and there traversing villages, but kept straight toward Jerusalem." Bede presses the deity in it: "let the Heathen cease to mock the Crucified as a mere man, who, as God, both foresaw the time of His crucifixion, and going voluntarily to be crucified, sought with steadfast face... the spot where He was to be crucified."

The witnesses note the phrase "received up" embraces the whole — passion, resurrection, and ascension — toward which Christ now sets his face with fixed resolve.

So the witnesses make the verse the solemn opening of Christ's journey to the cross. The lesson the expositors draw is the voluntary, foreknowing resolve with which Christ went to his passion. The witnesses note the weight of "the time was come that he should be received up" — the appointed hour drawing near for the whole of his exaltation through suffering: his death, his rising, and his ascension to glory. And Christ, the expositors press, "stedfastly set his face to go to Jerusalem" — not driven by his enemies, not stumbling unawares, but advancing with fixed and resolute purpose toward the city where he must die. Titus's note marks the fitness: the true Paschal Lamb must be offered at Jerusalem, where the typical lambs were slain; and Christ went straight there, not wandering, but setting his course directly to the place of sacrifice. Bede's word, the witnesses observe, draws out the deity: this steadfast advance to a foreseen death proves Christ no mere man overtaken by fate, but the God who foreknew his cross and went to it willingly — the voluntary self-offering of the Lamb. The "set his face," the expositors note, became proverbial for unflinching resolve (cf. Isaiah 50:7, "I set my face like a flint"); and so Christ, knowing all that awaited him, turned toward Jerusalem with an undaunted mind. The verse thus opens the long final journey of Luke's Gospel, marking the Saviour's deliberate, foreknowing, and willing march to the cross for the salvation of men.
52:
"And sent messengers before his face: and they went, and entered into a village of the Samaritans, to make ready for him." The witnesses note the embassy and the route through Samaria. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Clarke observe that "messengers" is literally "angels" (angeloi) — proof that "the word angel in the Bible does not always mean heavenly beings" but "a messenger of any kind." Barnes notes the practical wisdom: Christ "made provision beforehand, and thus has shown us that it is not improper to look out beforehand for the supply of our wants." Gill suggests the messengers "very likely were James and John, since they so highly resented the ill treatment." Lightfoot discusses the Jewish reluctance to lodge among Samaritans, noting it sprang from "mere hatred to the nation" rather than real danger of defilement.

The witnesses note that Christ, taking the direct route through Samaria, sent ahead to prepare lodging in a Samaritan village — an overture of peace to the people the Jews despised.

So the witnesses make the verse the sending of the embassy before Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, a point of language: the "messengers" are called "angels" in the Greek, which the witnesses note shows that the biblical word for "angel" means simply a messenger, whether human or heavenly — a useful corrective to the assumption that "angel" always denotes a celestial being. Second, the practical and gracious wisdom of Christ: he sent ahead to "make ready" — to arrange lodging and refreshment — and in this, Barnes notes, he sanctions reasonable forethought for our needs, not presuming on providence but making prudent provision. The witnesses note the significance of the route: Christ, going to Jerusalem, passed through Samaria, the country of a people the Jews shunned and hated; his sending messengers to a Samaritan village to prepare for him was itself an act of grace toward the despised, an overture that the Samaritans would spurn. Lightfoot's observation, the expositors observe, lays bare the depth of Jewish-Samaritan enmity: the reluctance to lodge among them was not from any real danger of defilement but from sheer national hatred. The verse thus sets the scene for the Samaritan rejection: Christ, in gracious condescension, sends to prepare lodging among a people who, in the next verse, would refuse him — and so prepares for the lesson on the spirit of his disciples that follows.
53:
"And they did not receive him, because his face was as though he would go to Jerusalem." The witnesses note the Samaritans' rejection and its cause. The grammatical-historical Barnes explains the reason: "one of the subjects of dispute between the Jews and Samaritans pertained to the proper situation of the temple. The Jews contended for Jerusalem; the Samaritans for Mount Gerizim... when they saw that he was going among the Jews — that by going he would decide in their favour — they resolved to have nothing to do with him." Gill suggests they had first received the messengers gladly, hoping Christ would honour them and approve their worship, but "when Christ came in person... they would not admit him."

The rejection, the witnesses note, sprang from sectarian pride: the Samaritans spurned Christ not for any fault, but because his journey to Jerusalem implied a verdict against their rival temple on Gerizim.

So the witnesses make the verse the Samaritans' inhospitable rejection of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the power of sectarian prejudice to close the heart against the Saviour. The Samaritans, the witnesses note, refused to receive Christ — not because of anything he had done or taught, but simply "because his face was as though he would go to Jerusalem." The expositors press the root: the long dispute between Jew and Samaritan over the proper place of worship — Jerusalem versus Mount Gerizim — so embittered the Samaritans that they would not lodge a traveller bound for the rival sanctuary. Had Christ been going to Gerizim, or had he decided their dispute in their favour, the witnesses note (with Gill), they would gladly have welcomed him, hoping he would honour their worship; but when his very destination declared him, as they thought, on the Jews' side, they rejected him. Barnes draws the abiding lessons: men wish all teachers to fall in with their own views; they readily reject a doctrine that does not serve their interest; and they spurn what seems to favour a rival party. The expositors observe the tragedy: the Samaritans shut out the very Saviour who, at Jacob's well, had offered living water to their own (John 4), letting their sectarian pride rob them of the One they should have received. The verse thus sets the offence that would provoke the rash zeal of James and John, and occasion Christ's rebuke — the Samaritans rejecting Christ for the sake of their temple, and the disciples answering rejection with a thirst for vengeance.
54:
"And when his disciples James and John saw this, they said, Lord, wilt thou that we command fire to come down from heaven, and consume them, even as Elias did?" The witnesses note the rash zeal of the sons of thunder. The grammatical-historical Barnes recalls their nickname: "they were called Boanerges, sons of thunder, probably on account of their vehement and rash zeal — a remarkable example of which we have in this instance" (Mark 3:17). He notes "the insult had been offered to Jesus, their friend, and they felt it; but their zeal was rash and their spirit bad. Vengeance belongs to God." Ellicott observes "the burning zeal of the sons of Zebedee, more fiery even than that of Peter," perhaps "heightened by the contrast with His former reception in a city of the same people" (John 4). Clarke notes they "referred to the case of Elijah" (2 Kings 1:10-12) to justify their zeal.

James and John, the witnesses note, indignant at the slight to their Master, asked leave to call down fire from heaven, as Elijah had done upon the soldiers of Ahaziah — a zeal real in its love but wholly wrong in its spirit.

So the witnesses make the verse a striking example of misguided zeal. The lesson the expositors draw is the danger of a zeal that is sincere yet carnal. James and John, the witnesses note, were moved by genuine love for Christ and indignation at the Samaritans' insult; their fault was not indifference but excess. Living up to their name, the "sons of thunder," they proposed to answer rejection with destruction — to call down fire from heaven and consume the inhospitable village. And they sought a precedent, the expositors observe: "even as Elias did," appealing to Elijah's calling down fire on Ahaziah's captains (2 Kings 1). The witnesses note the mixture in their request: a true attachment to Christ, but joined to a vengeful, intolerant spirit that mistook private resentment for holy zeal. Ellicott's suggestion, the expositors observe, adds context: their anger may have been sharpened by remembering how warmly Samaritans had once received Christ (John 4), making this rejection the more galling. But the deeper fault, the witnesses press, was the spirit itself — the desire to destroy those who would not receive their Lord, dressed up as zeal for his honour. The verse thus sets the rash request that Christ would rebuke, exposing how easily love for Christ may be corrupted into a revengeful temper, and how the appeal to scriptural precedent may cloak a spirit utterly opposed to the gospel.
55:
"But he turned, and rebuked them, and said, Ye know not what manner of spirit ye are of." The witnesses note Christ's rebuke and its searching diagnosis. Lightfoot draws out the contrast: "what Elias once did, the sons of Zebedee had an ambition to imitate, dreaming that there were those thunders and lightnings in their very name Boanerges... But could you not see, O sons of Zebedee, how careful and tender your Master was about the lives of mankind — how he healed the sick, raised the dead? and will you be breathing slaughter and fire?... you do not know what kind of spirit becomes the apostles of the Messiah." The grammatical-historical Barnes presses the self-examination: "you suppose you are actuated by a proper love for me; but you know not yourselves — it is rather a love of revenge... apparent zeal for God may be only improper opposition toward our fellow-men."

Christ, the witnesses note, "turned and rebuked them" — sharply reproving the very disciples who thought they were honouring him, exposing the unchristlike spirit behind their zeal.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's rebuke of false zeal and its abiding lesson. The lesson the expositors draw is the need to examine the spirit behind our supposed zeal for God. Christ, the witnesses note, did not commend the disciples' indignation but rebuked it, turning upon them with the searching word: "Ye know not what manner of spirit ye are of." The expositors press the depth of this. The disciples thought their zeal was love for Christ; but Christ discerned, beneath it, a spirit of revenge — a wrath against the Samaritans masquerading as devotion to him. Barnes draws the threefold lesson: apparent zeal for God may be merely ill-will toward men; those who would honour God should examine whether some bad feeling toward their fellows lies at the root of their professed zeal; and even the disciples nearest Christ could mistake a vengeful temper for holy fervour. Lightfoot's reflection, the witnesses observe, sharpens it by contrast: the disciples had seen their Master forever tender of human life — healing, raising the dead, sparing — and yet they proposed slaughter; they had failed utterly to catch his spirit. The expositors note the abiding warning: zeal is not self-validating; it must be tried by the spirit of Christ, who came not to destroy but to save. The verse thus turns the rebuke inward, teaching every disciple to search the temper behind his zeal, lest, like the sons of thunder, he serve revenge while imagining he serves God.
56:
"For the Son of man is not come to destroy men's lives, but to save them. And they went to another village." The witnesses note Christ's purpose of mercy and a significant textual question. The grammatical-historical Clarke and Ellicott observe that much of verses 55-56 is absent from the best manuscripts: Clarke notes "the words, Ye know not of what spirit ye are; for the Son of man is not come to destroy men's lives, but to save them, are wanting in [many manuscripts]... Griesbach leaves the latter clause out of the text," the most ancient reading being simply "But he turned and rebuked them. And they went to another village." Ellicott conjectures the words "were inserted from an oral tradition that had preserved what the Evangelist had omitted." Barnes (treating them as genuine in sense) draws the lesson: "He came not to destroy. If he had come for that purpose, he would have destroyed these Samaritans; but he came to save."

The witnesses note the substance, whatever its textual standing: Christ's mission is to save, not to destroy; and he simply withdrew to another village, answering rejection with patience.

So the witnesses make the verse the declaration of Christ's saving purpose and the model of his patience. The lesson the expositors draw is the contrast between the disciples' destructive zeal and the Saviour's mission of mercy. The textual point, the witnesses note, is real: many of the best manuscripts omit the explanatory words "Ye know not what spirit ye are of; for the Son of man is not come to destroy but to save," so that the oldest text reads simply that Christ rebuked them and passed on. Yet the sentiment, the expositors press, is thoroughly true to Christ and consonant with the whole Gospel — whether part of Luke's original or a faithful tradition preserving Christ's own words (Ellicott), it expresses the very heart of his coming: "the Son of man is not come to destroy men's lives, but to save them" (cf. John 3:17; 12:47). The witnesses note the force against the disciples' request: they would call down fire to destroy; Christ came to save the very people they would consume. And the quiet close — "they went to another village" — the expositors observe, shows the Saviour's patience: he neither avenged the insult nor lingered to argue, but withdrew, leaving the Samaritans to their refusal and seeking a more willing welcome elsewhere. The verse thus crowns the rebuke with the positive truth: Christ's errand is salvation, not destruction; and his response to rejection is not fire but forbearance — a pattern for his disciples, who are to seek the saving, not the ruin, of those who reject them.
57:
"And it came to pass, that, as they went in the way, a certain man said unto him, Lord, I will follow thee whithersoever thou goest." The witnesses note the first of three would-be followers and detect a flaw beneath the eager words. Cyril diagnoses presumption: "there is much tardiness implied... he is filled with too great presumption, for he sought not to follow Christ simply as others, but caught at the honor of the Apostleship," whereas "no one takes the honor to himself but he that is called of God" (Hebrews 5:4). Athanasius presses the rashness of "whithersoever thou goest": "he dared to match himself with the incomprehensible power of the Savior... to follow simply to hear His teaching is possible to human nature, but to go with Him wherever He is, is not possible, for He is incomprehensible." Theophylact suspects a mercenary motive: seeing Christ "drawing much people, he thought he received reward from them, and that if he followed, he might obtain money."

The man's offer, the witnesses note, was bold and seemingly devoted — to follow Christ anywhere — yet sprang from presumption, ambition, or hope of gain, not from a true reckoning of the cost.

So the witnesses make the verse the first lesson on discipleship's cost. The lesson the expositors draw is that eager words are not enough; the would-be follower must count the cost and come with a right heart. The man, the witnesses note, approached with a confident, even extravagant offer — "I will follow thee whithersoever thou goest" — but the fathers discern beneath it a flawed spirit. Cyril sees presumption: the man thrust himself forward, grasping at the honour of discipleship (even apostleship) which God bestows only on those he calls. Athanasius sees rashness: to vow to follow Christ "wherever" he goes overreaches, for Christ is more than a human teacher to be accompanied at will. Theophylact suspects a worldly motive: the man may have hoped, seeing the crowds, to profit by following so popular a master. The witnesses note that Christ neither flatly rejects nor warmly accepts him, but (in the next verse) tests him with the hard truth of his own homelessness — drawing out whether the man would follow a Lord who had nowhere to lay his head. The verse thus opens the threefold teaching on the cost of following Christ: discipleship is not to be undertaken on a wave of feeling or for hope of gain, but with sober reckoning of what it demands; and Christ will sift the eager volunteer by the poverty and self-denial of the way he calls men to walk.
58:
"And Jesus said unto him, Foxes have holes, and birds of the air have nests; but the Son of man hath not where to lay his head." The witnesses note Christ's testing answer. The grammatical-historical Gill observes the words are "exactly the same as in Matthew," whether the same occasion or a like one repeated. Wesley draws the lesson: "first understand the terms; consider on what conditions thou art to follow me." Theophylact (on v.57) had read it as answering the mercenary hope: Christ shows the man "why do you seek to follow Me for riches and gain?" — for the Lord himself had less than the foxes and birds.

Christ's reply, the witnesses note, does not refuse the man but sets before him the truth: to follow Christ is to share his homelessness and poverty, the Son of man having no settled dwelling, not even the shelter the beasts and birds enjoy.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's sobering test of the eager volunteer. The lesson the expositors draw is the poverty and self-denial that following Christ entails. The man had offered to follow Christ anywhere, the witnesses note, perhaps imagining gain or comfort in the train of so popular a master; and Christ answers by laying bare his own destitution: "the Son of man hath not where to lay his head." The expositors press the force: the foxes have their holes and the birds their nests — even the wild creatures have a settled place of rest — but the incarnate Lord, in his earthly ministry, had no fixed home, no certain shelter, living in poverty and dependence as he went about his Father's work. To follow such a Master, the witnesses note, is to embrace that same lot — to renounce the love of comfort and gain, and to be content with hardship for his sake. Wesley's word, the expositors observe, draws the practical point: count the terms before you vow; understand on what conditions you offer to follow Christ. The Lord does not deceive the eager with promises of ease, but tells him plainly the poverty of the way. The verse thus tests the would-be disciple's heart: if he sought Christ for gain or comfort, he is undeceived; if he would follow truly, he must be willing to share his Master's homelessness. And so the answer probes whether the man's eager words sprang from a heart ready for the self-denial that genuine discipleship demands.
59:
"And he said unto another, Follow me. But he said, Lord, suffer me first to go and bury my father." The witnesses note the second case — one called by Christ, who pleads delay. The grammatical-historical Gill notes this was "one of his disciples, who had attended him some time, and whom he now called to the ministerial work." Cyril of Alexandria frames the section as teaching how we follow Christ and are "counted worthy of those perfect honours first bestowed upon the apostles" — Christ, "the head and teacher of every virtue," calling men by the gospel into the path of the better life.

The man's request, the witnesses note, seems dutiful — to bury a father is a sacred filial obligation — yet it asks Christ's call to wait upon a delay, putting another duty "first."

So the witnesses make the verse the second lesson on discipleship: the call of Christ takes precedence over all. The lesson the expositors draw is the supreme claim of Christ's summons, before which even sacred duties must yield. Here, the witnesses note, the case is reversed from the first: Christ himself calls — "Follow me" — and the man, though willing, asks leave first to bury his father. The request, the expositors observe, appears reasonable, even pious: the burial of a parent was among the most sacred of duties, and filial reverence might seem to justify the delay. Yet the man's word "first" reveals the danger: he would put the claim of the dead before the call of the living Lord. The witnesses note the common interpretation (drawn out in the next verse): the plea may have been a pretext, the father perhaps not yet dead but aged, the man using filial duty to postpone an inconvenient call. Whatever the case, Christ's answer will press the principle that his summons admits no rival, no "first" — that to be called by Christ to the work of the kingdom is a claim above all earthly ties, however sacred. Cyril's framing, the witnesses observe, sets the dignity of the call: to follow Christ is to be drawn into the apostolic path of virtue, the highest honour, which must not be made to wait on lesser things. The verse thus poses the test of priority: when Christ calls, even the holiest duty must not be set "first" before him — and his reply will declare the urgency and supremacy of the gospel summons.
60:
"Jesus said unto him, Let the dead bury their dead: but go thou and preach the kingdom of God." The witnesses expound the famous paradox and the urgency of the call. The grammatical-historical Robertson explains: "the spiritually dead can bury the literally dead" — a "quick change in the use of the same words," as elsewhere in John. He judges "the harshness of this proverb to the scribe probably is due to the fact that he was manifestly using his aged father as an excuse for not giving Christ active service... Christ called him to preach, and he was using pious phrases about his father as a pretext." Lightfoot supplies a Jewish background: "the Jews accounted of the Gentiles as no other than dead," and even among themselves "the blind, the leprous, the poor, and the childless" were reckoned "as dead."

The witnesses note the sharp priority Christ sets: "go thou and preach the kingdom of God" — the call to active gospel service overriding the claim of the burial, which others (the "dead") could perform.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's assertion of the supreme urgency of the gospel call. The lesson the expositors draw is that the summons of Christ to his service takes precedence over every other claim, even the most sacred. The paradox "let the dead bury their dead," the witnesses note, turns on the double sense of "dead": let the spiritually dead — those without spiritual life — attend to the burying of the naturally dead, a task that does not require a living disciple of Christ; but the man called to preach has a higher, more urgent duty. Robertson's reading, the expositors press, likely explains the seeming harshness: the man was using his father (perhaps aged, not yet dead) as a pretext to postpone or evade an inconvenient call, cloaking reluctance in pious filial language; and Christ, reading the heart, cuts through the excuse with a sharp word. The witnesses note that Christ does not in general set aside filial duty — elsewhere he condemns those who neglect their parents (Mark 7:9-13) — but here, where the call to preach is clear and the plea a pretext, he presses the absolute priority of the kingdom. The expositors draw the abiding application: when God's call to his service is plain, no earthly tie, however dear, may be allowed to hinder or delay it; the preacher's duty is set sharply — "But go thou" — above all rival claims. The verse thus declares the supremacy and urgency of the gospel summons: the work of proclaiming the kingdom of God admits no postponement, and the called must go.
61:
"And another also said, Lord, I will follow thee; but let me first go bid them farewell, which are at home at my house." The witnesses note the third would-be follower and his backward-looking heart. Cyril of Alexandria contrasts the cases: the first man "was rejected as being self-called, and thrusting himself into honours"; but the right manner of following is taught by Christ's own call, "Follow me." This third man, the witnesses note, like the second, attaches a "first" — asking leave to return home and take leave of his household before following.

The request seems modest — only to say farewell — yet, the witnesses note, it betrays a heart still attached to home, looking back even as it offers to follow, and so unfit (as the next verse declares) for the kingdom.

So the witnesses make the verse the third lesson on the cost of following. The lesson the expositors draw is the danger of a divided heart that would follow Christ while still clinging to what it leaves behind. The third man, the witnesses note, resembles Elisha, who asked leave to kiss his parents before following Elijah (1 Kings 19:20) — and on the surface his request is reasonable, even courteous: only to bid farewell to his family before setting out. Yet Christ's answer (in the next verse) treats it as a fatal flaw, the expositors press, because it reveals a heart not wholly given: the man would follow, but "first" he must go back; his affections are still anchored at home, and his offer to follow is qualified by a backward look. The witnesses note the subtle peril: unlike the open pretext of the second man, this seems a small and innocent delay; but it discloses the same divided will — a desire to follow Christ without leaving all, to serve the kingdom while keeping one hand on the world. Cyril's framing, the expositors observe, sets the contrast: true following is at Christ's call and on his terms, not hedged about with conditions and farewells that betray reluctance. The verse thus presents the third case — the seemingly modest request that masks a heart still turned homeward — and prepares for Christ's decisive word: that he who puts his hand to the plough and looks back is not fit for the kingdom of God.
62:
"And Jesus said unto him, No man, having put his hand to the plough, and looking back, is fit for the kingdom of God." The witnesses expound the decisive word on wholehearted discipleship. The grammatical-historical Barnes explains the proverb: "to put one's hand to a plough is a proverbial expression to signify undertaking any business; in order that a ploughman may accomplish his work, it is necessary to look onward, not to be looking back with regret." He presses the application: "he that enters on religion must do it with his whole heart; he that comes still loving the world — still looking with regret on its pleasures, its wealth, its honours — cannot be a Christian... Religion is everything or nothing; he that is not willing to sacrifice everything for the cause of God is really willing to sacrifice nothing." Clarke applies it to the ministry: "can any person properly discharge the work of the ministry who is engaged in secular employments? A farmer and a minister of the Gospel are incompatible characters."

The image, the witnesses note, is vivid: the ploughman who looks back cannot drive a straight furrow; so the disciple whose heart turns back to what he has left cannot do the work of the kingdom.

So the witnesses make the verse the climax of the teaching on discipleship's cost. The lesson the expositors draw is that following Christ demands a whole and undivided heart, with no backward look. The proverb, the witnesses note, is drawn from the field: a man ploughing must fix his eyes forward, for if he looks back he cannot keep his furrow straight; the work requires single-minded attention. So it is, the expositors press, with the disciple: he who has set his hand to follow Christ must not look back with longing to the world, its pleasures, its ties, the home he has left. The third man's request to "first" bid farewell, the witnesses observe, was just such a backward look — a heart still turned homeward — and Christ declares that such a one "is not fit for the kingdom of God." Barnes's searching application, the expositors note, presses the radical claim: religion is "everything or nothing"; the half-hearted, who would follow Christ while still clinging to the world, are not Christians at all; true discipleship requires the readiness to give up all — property, comfort, even life — when God demands it. Clarke draws a particular lesson for the ministry: the called preacher cannot be entangled in secular pursuits, but must give himself wholly to the work. The witnesses note the gathering force of the three cases: against the presumptuous, the procrastinating, and the divided, Christ sets the one demand of wholehearted, forward-looking devotion. The verse thus seals the chapter's teaching on the cost of following: no man who looks back is fit for the kingdom; Christ will have the whole heart or none.
